content.statement: A set is a mathematical collection of objects, called elements.
content.definition: A collection considered as a single mathematical object.
content.formal: Set theory treats membership $x\in A$ as the primitive relation between an object $x$ and a set $A$.
content.intuition: The raw substrate of topology: a space starts as a set before any open sets are chosen.

content.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ assigns each element of its domain $X$ exactly one element of its codomain $Y$.
content.definition: A single-valued, total relation from a domain to a codomain.
content.formal: $f\subseteq X\times Y$ and for every $x\in X$ there exists a unique $y\in Y$ with $(x,y)\in f$.
content.intuition: A rule with no ambiguity: every input has exactly one output.

content.statement: The image of a function $f:X\to Y$ is the set of all outputs of $f$.
content.definition: The set of outputs of a function.
content.intuition: The set of values that the function actually takes on.

content.statement: The inverse of a function $f:X\to Y$ is the relation that reverses the ordered pairs of $f$. It is a function $Y\to X$ exactly when $f$ is bijective.
content.definition: The inverse relation obtained by swapping the inputs and outputs of a function; it becomes a genuine inverse function precisely for bijections.
content.formal: $f^{-1}=\{(y,x)\in Y\times X : (x,y)\in f\}.$
content.intuition: The inverse relation records how to move backward through $f$. Without bijectivity, one output may have no input or several inputs, so the backward relation need not be a function.
content.gloss: Do not confuse this inverse relation with the set preimage $f^{-1}(B)$; the same notation is overloaded by context.

examples.tex: If $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ is $f(x)=x+1$, then $f^{-1}(y)=y-1$. If $f(x)=x^2$, the inverse relation is not a function $\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$.

content.statement: The restriction of a function $f:X\to Y$ to a subset $A\subseteq X$ is the function obtained by limiting the domain of $f$ to $A$.
content.definition: The function obtained by limiting the domain of a function to a subset.
content.formal: $f|_A : A \to Y$ defined by $f|_A(a) = f(a)$ for all $a\in A$.
content.intuition: The same rule as the original function, but only applied to a smaller set of inputs.

content.statement: Every indexed family of nonempty sets has a choice function.
content.formal: If $(A_i)_{i\in I}$ and $A_i\ne\varnothing$ for all $i$, then there exists $c:I\to\bigcup_{i\in I}A_i$ such that $c(i)\in A_i$ for all $i$.
content.intuition: You may choose one element from each set in a family, even when there is no explicit rule for making the choices.

content.statement: The ambient space is Hausdorff.
The ambient space is normal.
The ambient space is regular.
The ambient space has a countable basis.
The ambient space is locally compact.
The metric space is complete.

A \textbf{topological space} is a pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ where $X$ is a set and $\mathcal{T}$ is a topology on $X$. The topology is the second component of the pair, and its elements are the open sets of the space.
content.definition: A pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ consisting of a set and a topology on it.
content.formal: A topological space is a pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ such that $X$ is a set and $\mathcal{T}\in\operatorname{Top}(X)$.
content.intuition: A topological space packages the underlying set together with its chosen topology.
content.gloss: The central object of topology: a set together with a choice of which subsets count as open.

examples.tex: Every metric space is a topological space via its metric topology (Thm. 3.2). On $\{a,b,c\}$, there are $29$ distinct topologies.

content.statement: A set with a pseudometric, allowing distinct points at distance zero.
content.definition: A set with a pseudometric, allowing distinct points at distance zero.

content.statement: A \textbf{metric space} $(X,d)$ is a non-empty set $X$ with a map $d\colon X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ such that for all $x,y,z\in X$: $$\begin{aligned} \textbf{(M1)}\quad d(x,y) &\ge 0,\ \text{and}\ d(x,y)=0\Leftrightarrow x=y \\ \textbf{(M2)}\quad d(x,y) &= d(y,x) \\ \textbf{(M3)}\quad d(x,z) &\le d(x,y)+d(y,z). \end{aligned}$$
content.definition: A set with a distance function satisfying positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
content.formal: Let $(M,d)$ with $d:M\times M\to[0,\infty)$, then $$\begin{align} d(x,y) &= 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x=y \\ d(x,y) &= d(y,x) \\ d(x,z) &\le d(x,y)+d(y,z) \end{align}$$
content.intuition: Topology with numerical distance still visible.
content.gloss: A set equipped with a notion of distance. The three axioms say distance is non-negative (and zero only between identical points), symmetric, and obeys the triangle inequality.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$ with $d(x,y)=\lVert x-y\rVert$ is the prototype. The \emph{discrete metric} ($d(x,y)=1$ for $x\neq y$, else $0$) makes any set a metric space.

content.statement: The discrete metric on a set $X$ is defined by $d(x,y)=1$ for $x\neq y$ and $d(x,x)=0$.
content.definition: A metric space where distinct points are at distance 1 and identical points are at distance 0.
content.formal: For a set $X$, the discrete metric $d$ is given by $d(x,y)=\begin{cases} 0 & \text{if }x=y \\ 1 & \text{if }x\neq y \end{cases}$
content.intuition: The most extreme metric, where every point is as far apart as possible from every other point.

content.statement: A subset equipped with the induced subspace topology.
content.definition: A subset equipped with the induced subspace topology.

content.statement: A Cartesian product equipped with the product topology.
content.definition: A Cartesian product equipped with the product topology.

content.statement: For a surjection $\pi\colon X\to A$ from a space $X$, the \textbf{quotient topology} on $A$ is $\{U\subseteq A\mid \pi^{-1}(U)\ \text{open in}\ X\}$. Then $\pi$ is a \textbf{quotient map} and $A$ the \textbf{quotient space}.
content.definition: A set of equivalence classes equipped with the quotient topology.
content.gloss: Glue points of $X$ together (via $\pi$) and declare a set in $A$ open exactly when its preimage is open. It is the finest topology making $\pi$ continuous.

examples.tex: Collapsing the boundary circle of a disc to a point yields a sphere; gluing the ends of $[0,1]$ yields $S^1$.

content.statement: $X$ is \textbf{compact} if every open cover of $X$ has a finite subcover.
content.definition: A topological space satisfying compactness.
content.gloss: No matter how you cover $X$ with open sets, finitely many already suffice. A powerful finiteness condition standing in for “closed and bounded.”

examples.tex: Finite spaces and indiscrete spaces are compact; $\mathbb{R}$ is not (the cover by $(n-1,n+1)$ has no finite subcover).

content.statement: A \textbf{separation} of $X$ is a pair of disjoint non-empty open sets $U,V$ with $U\cup V=X$. $X$ is \textbf{connected} if it has no separation; otherwise \textbf{disconnected}.
content.definition: A topological space satisfying connectedness.
content.gloss: Connected means the space cannot be split into two non-trivial open pieces — equivalently, its only clopen subsets are $\varnothing$ and $X$.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}$ is connected (Thm. 6.12); a two-point discrete space is disconnected.

content.statement: A \textbf{path} from $x$ to $y$ is a continuous map $f\colon[a,b]\to X$ with $f(a)=x$, $f(b)=y$. $X$ is \textbf{path-connected} if any two points are joined by a path.
content.definition: A topological space satisfying path connectedness.
content.gloss: You can walk continuously between any two points. A stronger, more geometric cousin of connectedness.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is path-connected, as is $\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{p\}$ for $n\ge 2$.

content.statement: $X$ is \textbf{Hausdorff} ($T_2$) if for all distinct $x,y\in X$ there are disjoint open sets $U\ni x$ and $V\ni y$.
content.definition: A topological space satisfying the Hausdorff property.
content.gloss: Distinct points can be “housed” in disjoint neighborhoods. This separation guarantees limits are unique and compact sets are closed.

examples.tex: Every metric space is Hausdorff; an infinite set with the cofinite topology is not.

content.statement: A regular space is a T1 topological space in which points and disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.definition: A topological space is regular if singletons are closed and every point can be separated from every closed set not containing it by disjoint open sets.
content.formal: $$(X,\tau)\text{ regular}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall x\in X\ \forall F\subseteq X,\ (F\text{ closed}\land x\notin F)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ F\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
content.intuition: Regularity says a point and a closed obstacle can be pulled apart topologically.

content.statement: A normal space is a T1 topological space in which disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.definition: A topological space is normal if singletons are closed and every pair of disjoint closed subsets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.formal: $$(X,\tau)\text{ normal}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall F,G\subseteq X,\ (F,G\text{ closed}\land F\cap G=\varnothing)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (F\subseteq U,\ G\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
content.intuition: Normality is closed-set separation: two closed pieces can be insulated from one another by open regions.

content.statement: $X$ is \textbf{locally compact} if every point has a compact neighborhood.
content.definition: A topological space is locally compact if every point has a neighborhood that is compact as a subspace. Under Hausdorff-type assumptions this is often expressed using open neighborhoods with compact closure.
content.formal: $$X\text{ locally compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists N\subseteq X\ \big(N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \land\ N\text{ is compact in the subspace topology}\big).$$
content.intuition: A space that is compact in the small — the natural setting for one-point compactification and for much of analysis.
content.gloss: Each point has a compact neighborhood, so the space looks compact near any given point.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$, every compact space, and every discrete space are locally compact; $\mathbb{Q}$ is not.

content.statement: A topological space with a countable basis.
content.definition: A topological space with a countable basis.

content.statement: An $n$-manifold is a Hausdorff, second-countable topological space locally homeomorphic to $\mathbb R^n$.
content.definition: A topological manifold without boundary is a Hausdorff, second-countable topological space such that every point has an open neighborhood homeomorphic to an open subset of $\mathbb R^n$.
content.formal: $$X\text{ is an }n\text{-manifold}\Longleftrightarrow X\text{ is Hausdorff and second-countable, and }\forall x\in X\ \exists U\ni x\ \exists V\subseteq\mathbb R^n\text{ open with }U\cong V.$$
content.intuition: A manifold is a space that may be globally curved or glued, but locally looks like ordinary Euclidean space.

content.statement: A space that is both compact and Hausdorff.
content.definition: A space that is both compact and Hausdorff.
content.intuition: The “best-behaved” compact spaces: points are separated, compact subsets are closed, and continuous bijections out of them are homeomorphisms.
content.gloss: Compactness plus the Hausdorff separation axiom — the combination behind many rigidity theorems.

examples.tex: $[0,1]$, $S^n$, and the torus $T^2$ are compact Hausdorff; a finite indiscrete space is compact but not Hausdorff.

content.statement: A topology on a set $X$ is a collection $\tau\subseteq\mathcal P(X)$ containing $\varnothing$ and $X$ and closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
content.definition: Given a set $X$, a topology $\tau$ on $X$ is a specified collection of subsets of $X$, called open sets, satisfying the topology axioms.
content.formal: $$ \begin{aligned} &\tau \subseteq \mathcal{P}(X), \qquad \varnothing, X \in \tau, \\ &\{U_i\}_{i \in I} \subseteq \tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i \in \tau, \\ &U_1,\ldots,U_n \in \tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bigcap_{k=1}^{n} U_k \in \tau. \end{aligned} $$
content.intuition: A topology is the open-set structure placed on a set. It is the collection $\tau$ itself, not the resulting space $(X,\tau)$.

content.statement: In a topological space $(X, \mathcal{T})$, a subset $U$ of $X$ is open exactly when $U$ belongs to $\mathcal{T}$.
content.definition: An open set of a topological space (X, τ) is a subset U⊆X that is an element of the topology τ.
content.formal: $$U\subseteq X \text{ is open in }(X,\mathcal{T})\Longleftrightarrow U\in\mathcal{T}.$$
content.intuition: Open sets are the primitive observable neighborhoods of the space; all topological notions are expressed in terms of them.

content.statement: A subset C of a topological space X is closed exactly when its complement in X is open.
content.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a subset C⊆X is closed in X if its complement X\C is open in X.
content.formal: $$C\subseteq X \text{ is closed in }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow X\setminus C\in\tau.$$
content.intuition: Closed sets are the subsets whose outside is topologically open; they contain their limit behavior as far as the topology detects it.
content.gloss: Closed = complement is open. Closed sets satisfy the dual axioms: arbitrary intersections and finite unions of closed sets are closed (Thm. 3.28).

examples.tex: In a discrete space every subset is closed; in $\mathbb{R}$ every interval $[a,b]$ and every singleton is closed.

content.statement: A subset N of a topological space X is a neighborhood of x if it contains an open set that contains x.
content.definition: In a topological space $(X,\mathcal T)$, a subset $N\subseteq X$ is a neighborhood of $x\in X$ if there exists an open set $U\in\mathcal T$ such that $x\in U\subseteq N$.
content.formal: $$N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\Longleftrightarrow \exists U\in\mathcal T\ \text{such that}\ x\in U\subseteq N.$$
content.intuition: A neighborhood is any set large enough to contain a genuinely open region around the point; it does not have to be open itself under the broad convention.
content.gloss: Broad convention: a neighborhood may be non-open, as long as it contains an open set around the point. When the set itself is open, call it an open neighborhood.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, $(-1,1)$ is a neighborhood of $0$ because $0\in(-1,1)$ and $(-1,1)$ is open.

content.statement: A basis for an existing topology is a collection of open sets such that every open set is a union of basis elements.
content.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a basis is a collection B of open subsets of X such that for every open set U and every point x∈U, there is B∈B with x∈B⊆U. Equivalently, every open set is a union of basis elements.
content.formal: $$\mathcal B\subseteq\tau\text{ is a basis for }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow \forall U\in\tau\ \forall x\in U\ \exists B\in\mathcal B\ (x\in B\subseteq U).$$
content.intuition: A basis gives small reusable open pieces from which all open sets are assembled by unions.
content.gloss: A small kit of sets from which all open sets are built as unions. Open $=$ union of basis elements (Thm. 4.10).

examples.tex: The open $\epsilon$-balls form a basis for the metric topology; all singletons form a basis for the discrete topology.

content.statement: A subbasis is a collection of subsets whose finite intersections form a basis for the topology it generates.
content.definition: A subbasis is a collection of subsets whose finite intersections form a basis for the topology generated by it; arbitrary unions of those finite intersections are the open sets.
content.formal: $$\tau(\mathcal S)=\left\{\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\bigcap_{k=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,k}:S_{\lambda,k}\in\mathcal S,\ n_\lambda<\infty\right\}.$$
content.intuition: A subbasis specifies even simpler open constraints; finite intersections make basic opens, and arbitrary unions make all opens.
content.gloss: An even smaller seed: take finite intersections to get a basis, then unions to get the topology. Open sets are unions of finite intersections of subbasis elements.

examples.tex: $\langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\big\{\bigcup_\lambda\bigcap_{i=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,i}\mid S_{\lambda,i}\in\mathcal{S}\big\}$ (Thm. 4.15).

content.statement: The closure of A in X is the smallest closed subset of X containing A.
content.definition: For A⊆X, the closure cl_X(A) is the intersection of all closed subsets of X that contain A; equivalently, it is the least closed subset of X containing A.
content.formal: $$\operatorname{cl}_X(A)=\overline A=\bigcap\{F\subseteq X: A\subseteq F\ \text{and}\ F \text{ is closed in }X\}.$$
content.intuition: The closure adds all points that cannot be separated from A by open neighborhoods.
content.gloss: The closure adds exactly the points $A$ “almost reaches.” If $A$ is already closed then $\overline{A}=A$.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$, $\overline{(a,b]}=[a,b]$. In general $\mathrm{Int}(A)\subseteq A\subseteq\overline{A}$.

content.statement: For a subset A of a topological space X, the interior of A is the largest open subset of X contained in A.
content.definition: Let $(X,\tau_X)$ be a topological space and let $A\subseteq X$. The interior of $A$ in $X$, denoted $\operatorname{int}_X(A)$ or $A^\circ$, is the union of all open subsets of $X$ contained in $A$. Equivalently, it is the largest open subset of $X$ contained in $A$.
content.formal: $$\operatorname{int}_X(A)=A^\circ=\bigcup\{U\in\tau_X:U\subseteq A\}.$$
content.intuition: The interior keeps exactly the points of $A$ that have room around them: a point $x$ lies in $\operatorname{int}_X(A)$ precisely when some open neighborhood of $x$ is contained entirely in $A$. Boundary points fail this test because every open neighborhood around them leaks outside $A$.
content.gloss: Dual to closure: closure expands $A$ by adding unavoidable nearby points, while interior trims $A$ down to the points it surrounds with open room. Always $$\operatorname{int}_X(A)\subseteq A\subseteq\overline{A}.$$

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$ with its usual topology, $$\operatorname{int}_{\mathbb{R}}((a,b])=(a,b).$$

content.statement: The boundary of A is the part of the closure of A outside the interior of A.
content.definition: For A⊆X, the boundary ∂_X A is cl_X(A)\int_X(A). Equivalently, it is the set of points whose every neighborhood meets both A and X\A.
content.formal: $$\partial_X A=\overline A\setminus A^\circ=\overline A\cap\overline{X\setminus A}.$$
content.intuition: Boundary points are exactly the points where the topology cannot see a whole neighborhood lying entirely inside A or entirely outside A.

content.statement: A subset A of X is dense in X exactly when its closure is all of X.
content.definition: For a topological space X, a subset A⊆X is dense in X if cl_X(A)=X. Equivalently, every nonempty open subset of X intersects A.
content.formal: $$A\subseteq X \text{ is dense in }X\Longleftrightarrow \overline A=X\Longleftrightarrow \forall\,\varnothing\ne U\in\tau,\ U\cap A\ne\varnothing.$$
content.intuition: A dense subset is topologically unavoidable: every nonempty open region of the space contains a point of it.
content.gloss: A dense set is everywhere — its closure fills the whole space, so it meets every open set.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$. In a discrete space the only dense subset is the whole space.

content.statement: A map between topological spaces is continuous exactly when inverse images of open sets are open.
content.definition: Given topological spaces (X, τ_X) and (Y, τ_Y), a function f:X→Y is continuous if the preimage of every open subset of Y is open in X.
content.formal: $$f:(X,\tau_X)\to(Y,\tau_Y)\text{ is continuous}\Longleftrightarrow \forall V\in\tau_Y,\ f^{-1}(V)\in\tau_X.$$
content.intuition: Continuity means that topological tests made by open sets in the codomain pull back to valid open tests in the domain.
content.gloss: Continuity means that open regions in the target pull back to open regions in the domain. This is the full topological definition: it uses only the open-set structure, not distances, balls, or a metric.

examples.tex: Every constant map is continuous, since the preimage of an open set is either $\varnothing$ or all of $X$. Every map from a discrete topological space is continuous, since every subset of the domain is open.

content.statement: A homeomorphism is a bijective continuous map whose inverse is also continuous.
content.definition: For topological spaces X and Y, a homeomorphism is a bijection f:X→Y such that both f and its inverse f^{-1}:Y→X are continuous. If such a map exists, X and Y are homeomorphic.
content.formal: $$f:X\to Y\text{ is a homeomorphism}\Longleftrightarrow f\text{ is bijective},\ f\text{ is continuous},\ \text{and }f^{-1}:Y\to X\text{ is continuous}.$$
content.intuition: A homeomorphism is an isomorphism of topological spaces: it preserves and reflects exactly the open-set structure.
content.gloss: The notion of sameness in topology: two spaces are homeomorphic when one can be transformed into the other by a reversible continuous deformation. their open-set structures correspond perfectly in both directions.

examples.tex: The map $f\colon \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=2x-1$, is a homeomorphism with inverse $f^{-1}(y)=\frac{y+1}{2}$. A continuous bijection need \emph{not} be a homeomorphism: if $X$ has at least two points, then $\mathrm{id}\colon X_{\mathrm{disc}}\to X_{\mathrm{ind}}$ is continuous and bijective, but its inverse is not continuous.

content.statement: A net converges to x when it is eventually contained in every neighborhood of x.
content.definition: In a topological space X, a net (x_i) indexed by a directed set I converges to x∈X if for every neighborhood N of x, the net is eventually in N. For sequences, I=ℕ with its usual order.
content.formal: $$(x_i)_{i\in I}\to x\Longleftrightarrow \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists i_0\in I\ \forall i\ge i_0,\ x_i\in N.$$
content.intuition: Convergence means that every topological test neighborhood of the proposed limit eventually contains all later terms.

content.statement: A topological space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover.
content.definition: Let $(X,\mathcal T)$ be a topological space. A collection $\mathcal U\subseteq\mathcal T$ is an open cover of $X$ if $X=\bigcup_{U\in\mathcal U}U$. The space $(X,\mathcal T)$ is compact if every open cover $\mathcal U$ of $X$ contains a finite subcover.
content.formal: $$(X,\mathcal T)\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal U\subseteq\mathcal T,\ \left(X\subseteq\bigcup_{U\in\mathcal U}U\Rightarrow \exists U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\mathcal U\text{ such that }X\subseteq\bigcup_{k=1}^{n}U_k\right).$$
content.intuition: Compactness says that whenever the whole space is described by possibly infinitely many open pieces, finitely many of those pieces already describe the whole space. It is a topological finiteness condition, not a size condition.
content.gloss: No matter how you cover $X$ with open sets, finitely many already suffice. A topological stand-in for “finite” — not about size, but about no escaping to infinity.
notation: $$\begin{aligned} \mathcal U&=\text{an open cover of }X,\\ \mathcal U_0&=\{U_1,\dots,U_n\}\subseteq\mathcal U=\text{a finite subcover},\\ X&=U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_n. \end{aligned}$$

examples.tex: A closed interval $[a,b]$ is compact; the open interval $(0,1)$ is not — the cover $\{(1/n,1)\}_{n\ge 2}$ has no finite subcover.

content.statement: A space is compact exactly when every closed family with the finite intersection property has nonempty total intersection.
content.definition: The finite intersection property characterization says that compactness is equivalent to the following closed-set condition: whenever a family of closed subsets has nonempty intersection for every finite subfamily, the whole family has nonempty intersection.
content.formal: $$X\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal F\subseteq\{F\subseteq X:F\text{ closed}\},\ \left[\left(\forall F_1,\ldots,F_n\in\mathcal F,\ \bigcap_{k=1}^{n}F_k\ne\varnothing\right)\Rightarrow \bigcap_{F\in\mathcal F}F\ne\varnothing\right].$$
content.intuition: This is the closed-set dual of the open-cover definition: failure of a finite subcover corresponds to finite intersections remaining nonempty.
content.gloss: The closed-set dual of compactness: shrinking closed sets that never disjointly vanish in finite batches must share a common point.
notation: $$\mathcal F$$ has the finite intersection property (FIP)

examples.tex: On compact $[0,1]$, nested closed intervals $[0,1/n]$ have the FIP and indeed intersect in $\{0\}$.

content.statement: A topological space is sequentially compact when every sequence has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in the space.
content.definition: A topological space X is sequentially compact if every sequence in X has a subsequence that converges to a point of X.
content.formal: $$X\text{ sequentially compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall (x_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\in X^{\mathbb N},\ \exists n_1<n_2<\cdots,\ \exists x\in X\text{ such that }x_{n_k}\to x.$$
content.intuition: Sequential compactness is a sequence-based finiteness property; in general topology it is not equivalent to open-cover compactness without additional hypotheses.
content.gloss: Every sequence has a subsequence settling down to a point of the space. Equivalent to compactness for metric spaces, but not in general.

examples.tex: $[0,1]$ is sequentially compact (Bolzano–Weierstrass); the product $[0,1]^{[0,1]}$ is compact yet not sequentially compact.

content.statement: A topological space is connected when it cannot be separated into two disjoint nonempty open subsets.
content.definition: A topological space X is connected if there do not exist disjoint nonempty open subsets U,V⊆X whose union is X. Equivalently, the only subsets of X that are both open and closed are ∅ and X.
content.formal: $$X\text{ connected}\Longleftrightarrow \nexists U,V\in\tau\ \text{with }U,V\ne\varnothing,\ U\cap V=\varnothing,\ \text{and }X=U\cup V.$$
content.intuition: Connectedness means the topology does not split the whole space into two separated open pieces.

content.statement: A topological space is path connected when every two points can be joined by a continuous path.
content.definition: A topological space X is path connected if for every pair of points x,y∈X there is a continuous map γ:[0,1]→X from the unit interval to X with γ(0)=x and γ(1)=y.
content.formal: $$X\text{ path connected}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ \exists \gamma:[0,1]\to X\text{ continuous with }\gamma(0)=x,\ \gamma(1)=y.$$
content.intuition: Path connectedness is connectedness witnessed by actual continuous paths between points; it is stronger than connectedness in general.

content.statement: Separation axioms are topological conditions that distinguish points and closed sets by open sets or neighborhoods.
content.definition: Separation axioms are a family of properties, including T0, T1, Hausdorff/T2, regularity, and normality, that measure how well a topology separates points from points or points/closed sets from closed sets using open sets.
content.formal:
$$T_0: orall x
e y\,\exists U\in	au\,[(x\in U\land y
otin U)\lor(y\in U\land x
otin U)],\quad T_2: orall x
e y\,\exists U,V\in	au\,(x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
content.intuition: They quantify how much the topology can distinguish different points or closed regions.

content.statement: A topological space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.definition: A topological space X is Hausdorff, or T2, if for every pair of distinct points x,y∈X there are disjoint open sets U and V with x∈U and y∈V.
content.formal: $$(X,\mathcal T)\text{ is Hausdorff}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ x\ne y\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\mathcal T\ \text{such that }x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing.$$
content.intuition: Hausdorffness says distinct points can be topologically pulled apart by nonoverlapping open regions.

content.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is regular if points and disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is regular if singletons are closed and, whenever F is closed and x∉F, there are disjoint open sets U,V with x∈U and F⊆V.
content.formal: $$(X,\tau)\text{ regular}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall x\in X\ \forall F\subseteq X,\ (F\text{ closed}\land x\notin F)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ F\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
content.intuition: Regularity separates a point from a closed obstacle by disjoint open regions.

content.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is normal if disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
content.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is normal if singletons are closed and every pair of disjoint closed subsets F,G⊆X has disjoint open neighborhoods U,V with F⊆U and G⊆V.
content.formal: $$(X,\tau)\text{ normal}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall F,G\subseteq X,\ (F,G\text{ closed}\land F\cap G=\varnothing)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (F\subseteq U,\ G\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).$$
content.intuition: Normality is a strong separation property for closed sets, not just for individual points.

content.statement: Countability axioms require bases or local neighborhood bases to be countable.
content.definition: Countability axioms are topological size restrictions on local or global bases, most commonly first countability and second countability.
content.formal: $$\text{First countable: }\forall x\in X\,\exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ (|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \text{and }\mathcal B_x\text{ is local at }x);\quad \text{Second countable: }\exists\mathcal B\text{ basis for }\tau,\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0.$$
content.intuition: They say that the topology can be probed using only countably many local or global open pieces.

content.statement: A space is first countable if each point has a countable neighborhood basis.
content.definition: A topological space X is first countable if for every point x∈X there is a countable collection of neighborhoods of x such that every neighborhood of x contains one of them.
content.formal: $$X\text{ first countable}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ \left(|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \land\ \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists B\in\mathcal B_x,\ x\in B\subseteq N\right).$$
content.intuition: First countability gives each point a countable local scale of neighborhoods, making sequences more informative.

content.statement: A space is second countable if its topology has a countable basis.
content.definition: A topological space (X, τ) is second countable if there exists a countable basis for τ.
content.formal: $$X\text{ second countable}\Longleftrightarrow \exists\mathcal B\subseteq\tau\ \left(\mathcal B\text{ is a basis for }\tau\ \land\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0\right).$$
content.intuition: Second countability gives a countable global vocabulary for all open sets.

content.statement: A space is locally compact if every point has a compact neighborhood.
content.definition: A topological space X is locally compact if each point x∈X has a neighborhood N that is compact in the subspace topology. In Hausdorff settings this is often equivalently expressed using neighborhoods with compact closure.
content.formal: $$X\text{ locally compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \text{such that }N\text{ is compact as a subspace of }X.$$
content.intuition: Local compactness means that around every point there is a topologically finite region, even if the whole space is not compact.
content.gloss: Compactness, but only locally: every point sits inside some compact neighborhood, even if the whole space runs off to infinity.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is locally compact (each point has a compact ball) but not compact; $\mathbb{Q}$ is not locally compact.

content.statement: For $A\subseteq X$, the \textbf{subspace topology} on $A$ is $\mathcal{T}_A=\{A\cap U\mid U\ \text{open in}\ X\}$.
content.definition: Open sets are A∩U where U is open in X.
content.gloss: $A$ inherits openness by slicing the open sets of $X$. A set may be open in $A$ without being open in $X$.

examples.tex: $K\subseteq A$ is closed in $A$ iff $K=A\cap L$ for some $L$ closed in $X$ (Thm. 5.3).

content.statement: For spaces $X,Y$, the \textbf{product topology} on $X\times Y$ is generated by the basis $\mathcal{B}=\{U\times V\mid U\ \text{open in}\ X,\ V\ \text{open in}\ Y\}$.
content.definition: Topology generated by inverse images of opens under coordinate projections.
content.gloss: Open sets of the product are unions of open rectangles. It is the coarsest topology making both projections continuous (its universal property).
notation: $$\prod_{i\in I} X_i$$ denotes the product of indexed spaces.

examples.tex: If $\mathcal{B}_X,\mathcal{B}_Y$ are bases, the products $B_X\times B_Y$ form a basis (Thm. 5.9). The torus $T^2=S^1\times S^1$ carries this topology.

content.statement: For a surjection $q:X\to Y$, the quotient topology on $Y$ is the topology in which $U\subseteq Y$ is open exactly when $q^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$.
content.definition: The quotient topology on the target of a surjection q:X→Y is the finest topology on Y making q continuous; equivalently, U⊆Y is open exactly when q^{-1}(U) is open in X.
content.formal: $$\tau_Y=\{U\subseteq Y:q^{-1}(U)\in\tau_X\}.$$
content.intuition: The quotient topology declares precisely the open sets whose full preimage was already open before gluing points together.

content.statement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Define $$\mathcal{T}_d=\left\{U\subseteq X\;\middle|\;\forall x\in U,\ \exists r>0\text{ such that }B(x;r)\subseteq U\right\}.$$ Then $\mathcal{T}_d$ is a topology on $X$. every metric space $(X,d)$ canonically determines a topological space $(X,\mathcal{T}_d)$, where the open sets are exactly those subsets whose every point contains a sufficiently small metric ball inside the subset.
content.definition: Topology generated by open metric balls.
content.gloss: A metric gives a notion of distance, and distance gives a notion of openness: a set is open when every one of its points has some room around it that stays inside the set. The topology axioms follow because unions preserve local room, while finite intersections preserve local room by taking the smallest available radius.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$ with $d(x,y)=|x-y|$, the metric topology is the usual topology generated by open intervals. In a discrete metric space, every subset is open, so the metric topology is the discrete topology.

steps.content: Clearly $\varnothing\in\mathcal{T}_d$. Also $X\in\mathcal{T}_d$ because every $x\in X$ has a metric ball contained in $X$.

If $V=\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}U_\lambda$ with each $U_\lambda\in\mathcal{T}_d$ and $x\in V$, then $x\in U_\lambda$ for some $\lambda$. A ball around $x$ contained in $U_\lambda$ is also contained in $V$.
If $x\in U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n$, choose radii $r_i>0$ with $\mathsf{B}(x;r_i)\subseteq U_i$. Taking $r=\min_i r_i$ gives $\mathsf{B}(x;r)\subseteq U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n$.
The metric-open subsets satisfy the topology axioms, so they define the metric topology $\mathcal{T}_d$.

content.statement: Topology generated by intervals and rays in a linear order.
content.definition: Topology generated by intervals and rays in a linear order.

content.statement: Adjoin a single point $\infty$ to a locally compact Hausdorff space $X$ to obtain a compact space $X^+ = X \cup \{\infty\}$.
content.definition: Adjoin one point at infinity to a locally compact noncompact space.
content.intuition: Wrap up the “escape to infinity” into one extra point so that the result becomes compact.
content.gloss: Add one point at infinity; open sets around it are complements of compact sets of $X$.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$ compactifies to the sphere $S^n$; the plane $\mathbb{C}$ becomes the Riemann sphere.

content.statement: The universal compact Hausdorff space $\beta X$ receiving a dense embedding of a Tychonoff space $X$, through which every bounded continuous map factors.
content.definition: Universal compact Hausdorff compactification for suitable spaces.
content.intuition: The “largest” compactification: every continuous map from $X$ to a compact Hausdorff space extends uniquely to $\beta X$.
content.gloss: The universal compactification — bounded continuous functions on $X$ extend to $\beta X$.

examples.tex: $\beta\mathbb{N}$ is the space of ultrafilters on $\mathbb{N}$; far larger than the one-point compactification $\mathbb{N}\cup\{\infty\}$.

content.statement: Coproduct topology making inclusions continuous.
content.definition: Coproduct topology making inclusions continuous.

content.statement: The finest topology making a family of maps into the space continuous.
content.definition: The finest topology making a family of maps into the space continuous.

content.statement: The coarsest topology making a family of maps out of the space continuous.
content.definition: The coarsest topology making a family of maps out of the space continuous.

content.statement: A family $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ of open sets covering X.
content.definition: A family {U_i}_{i∈I} of open sets covering X.

content.statement: A proof method that begins with an arbitrary open cover and uses the topology of the space to extract a useful subcover or local refinement.
content.definition: A compactness-style proof pattern based on arbitrary open covers.
content.intuition: To prove compactness, never choose a convenient cover; start with an arbitrary one and force a finite subcover.

content.statement: A proof method that shows finitely many sets from a cover still cover the object of interest.
content.definition: The standard finite-extraction step in compactness arguments.
content.intuition: This is the moment where compactness converts infinite-looking data into finite data.

content.statement: A proof method that separates points, closed sets, or compact sets using open neighborhoods or continuous functions.
content.definition: The standard proof pattern for separation axioms and Urysohn/Tietze-type results.
content.intuition: Show two objects can be insulated from each other by open sets or functions.

content.statement: A proof method that reduces an open-set statement to basis or subbasis elements.
content.definition: A topology proof pattern using basis/subbasis generators instead of arbitrary open sets.
content.intuition: Prove the claim on small generating pieces, then extend by unions and finite intersections.

content.statement: If every chain in a partially ordered set has an upper bound, then the poset has a maximal element.
content.formal: For a poset $(P,\le)$, if every chain $C\subseteq P$ has an upper bound in $P$, then $P$ contains a maximal element.
content.intuition: A maximality principle equivalent to the Axiom of Choice; often used to build maximal covers, bases, ideals, or filters.

concepts.label: Compact Subsets of Hausdorff Spaces Are Closed

content.statement: In a Hausdorff space, compact subsets are closed.
content.intuition: In a Hausdorff space, compactness forces closedness — a frequently used bridge between the two notions.
content.gloss: In a Hausdorff space, compactness forces closedness — a frequently used bridge between the two notions.

examples.tex: Combined with Thm. 6.37 it shows a continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is a homeomorphism.

steps.content: To prove $K$ is closed, show $K^c=X\setminus K$ is open. Fix $x\in K^c$.
For each $y\in K$, Hausdorffness gives disjoint neighborhoods $U_y$ of $x$ and $V_y$ of $y$.
The family $\{V_y:y\in K\}$ covers $K$, so compactness gives finitely many $V_{y_1},\ldots,V_{y_n}$ covering $K$.
Let $U=U_{y_1}\cap\cdots\cap U_{y_n}$. Then $U$ is an open neighborhood of $x$ disjoint from the finite union covering $K$, so $U\subseteq K^c$. Hence $K^c$ is open.

content.statement: A closed simply connected 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere.
Use topology to formulate convergence, continuity, compactness, and fixed points in metric settings.
Use circles, tori, and Hawaiian earrings as testing spaces.
A subset $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ (Euclidean) is compact iff it is closed and bounded.
content.formal: For $A\subseteq\mathbb R^n$ with the Euclidean topology, $A$ is compact if and only if $A$ is closed and bounded.
content.intuition: Heine-Borel identifies abstract open-cover compactness in Euclidean space with the geometric condition of fitting inside a finite box and containing all limit points.
content.gloss: In Euclidean space, the abstract notion of compactness coincides exactly with the concrete “closed and bounded.” One of the cornerstones of analysis.

examples.tex: Closed intervals $[a,b]$, closed balls, and spheres in $\mathbb R^n$ are compact; $(0,1)$ is bounded but not compact, and $\mathbb R$ is closed but not compact.

steps.content: If $A$ is compact, then $\mathbb{R}^n$ is Hausdorff, so compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ are closed.
Cover $A$ by centered balls $\mathsf{B}(0;n)$. A finite subcover gives $A\subseteq\mathsf{B}(0;N)$ for some $N$, so $A$ is bounded.
If $A$ is closed and bounded, then $A$ lies in a product of closed bounded intervals, which is compact.
An open cover of $A$ can be enlarged by $A^c$ to cover the compact box. A finite subcover restricts to a finite subcover of $A$, so $A$ is compact.

content.statement: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has a convergent subsequence.
content.formal: Let $(x_k)$ be a bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Then there is a subsequence $(x_{k_j})$ and a point $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$ such that $x_{k_j}\to x$.
content.intuition: Bolzano-Weierstrass is the sequence form of compactness in Euclidean space: bounded infinite data has a convergent subsequence.
content.gloss: Every bounded sequence in Euclidean space has a convergent subsequence. This is the sequential form of compactness for $\mathbb{R}^n$, translating the abstract topological notion into concrete analytic terms.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$, the sequence $(1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, \ldots)$ is bounded, so it has a convergent subsequence (the whole sequence converges to 0). A sequence like $(1, 2, 3, \ldots)$ is unbounded and need not have a convergent subsequence.

content.statement: In a normal space, disjoint closed sets can be separated by a continuous real-valued function.
content.formal: Let $X$ be normal and let $A,B\subseteq X$ be disjoint closed subsets. Then there exists a continuous function $f:X\to[0,1]$ with $f(A)=\{0\}$ and $f(B)=\{1\}$.
content.intuition: Urysohn lemma turns normality, a separation property for closed sets, into continuous functions.

content.statement: A continuous real-valued function on a closed subset of a normal space extends continuously to the whole space.
content.formal: Let $X$ be normal, let $A\subseteq X$ be closed, and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous (bounded, in the standard bounded form). Then $f$ extends to a continuous function $F:X\to\mathbb{R}$.
content.intuition: Tietze extension upgrades normal separation into an extension theorem for continuous functions.

content.statement: A subset of a topological space is compact if and only if every cover by subbasic open sets has a finite subcover.
content.formal: Let $X$ be a topological space with subbase $\mathcal{S}$. A subset $K \subseteq X$ is compact if and only if for every cover of $K$ consisting of sets in $\mathcal{S}$, there exists a finite subcover.
content.intuition: Alexander's subbase lemma transforms compactness—an intrinsically global property about arbitrary open covers—into a testable condition on a much smaller subbasis. This is powerful because it reduces the verification burden from checking all open covers to checking only subbasic covers.
content.gloss: The pivotal compactness shortcut: to prove a space is compact, it suffices to check that every cover by subbasic open sets has a finite subcover, rather than checking all possible open covers. This dramatically simplifies compactness proofs and is the key ingredient in proving Tychonoff's theorem.

examples.tex: In the product topology, cylinder sets (inverse images of open sets in one factor) form a subbasis. Alexander's lemma shows that to prove a product is compact, one needs only to handle such subbasic covers—which is much more tractable than handling all open covers.

steps.content: If $K$ is compact, then every subbasic cover is an open cover, so it has a finite subcover.
Conversely, assume every subbasic cover has a finite subcover but $K$ is not compact. Use Zorn's lemma to choose a maximal open cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $K$ with no finite subcover.
Since $\mathcal{C}$ covers $K$ but $\mathcal{C}\cap\mathcal{S}$ cannot already cover $K$, choose $x$ and then an open $U\in\mathcal{C}$ containing $x$, represented using finitely many subbasic sets.
Adding any missing subbasic piece to $\mathcal{C}$ creates a finite subcover by maximality. Combining finitely many such covers gives a finite subcover from $\mathcal{C}$, contradiction.

content.statement: The product of any family of compact spaces is compact in the product topology.
content.formal: If $\{X_i\}_{i\in I}$ is a family of compact spaces, then $\prod_{i\in I} X_i$ is compact in the product topology.
content.intuition: Tychonoff theorem is the central compactness theorem for arbitrary products; standard proofs use Alexander subbase theorem and choice.
content.gloss: Products of compact spaces are compact. A fundamental rigidity principle in topology: compactness persists under arbitrary products (with the axiom of choice), making product spaces of compact factors automatically compact. Indispensable for functional analysis and abstract algebra.

examples.tex: The Cantor cube $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ is compact as a product of countably many copies of the compact space $\{0,1\}$. The Hilbert cube $[0,1]^{\mathbb{N}}$ (a product of countably many copies of $[0,1]$) is also compact.

steps.content: Apply Alexander's subbase lemma to the canonical subbasis of the product topology.
If compactness failed, Zorn's lemma supplies a maximal open cover with no finite subcover.
Subbasic sets constrain one coordinate at a time; failure of a finite subcover would induce an open cover of a compact factor with no finite subcover.
Compactness of each factor gives the needed finite subcover, contradicting the maximal bad cover.

content.statement: Complete metric spaces and locally compact Hausdorff spaces are Baire spaces: countable intersections of dense open sets are dense.
content.formal: If $X$ is a complete metric space, or more generally a locally compact Hausdorff space in the listed variant, then every countable intersection of dense open subsets of $X$ is dense.
content.intuition: Baire category says that these spaces cannot be built as a thin countable union of nowhere dense pieces.

content.statement: Every regular second-countable Hausdorff space is metrizable.
content.formal: If $X$ is regular, second countable, and Hausdorff, then there exists a metric $d$ on $X$ whose metric topology is the original topology.
content.intuition: Urysohn metrization gives conditions under which abstract open-set topology comes from an actual metric.

content.statement: Let $f: \mathbb{D}^n \to \mathbb{D}^n$ be a continuous map from the (unit) disk $\mathbb{D}^n$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to itself. Then $f$ has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point $x \in \mathbb{D}^n$ such that $f(x) = x$. For $n = 1$ this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map $f: [0, 1] \to [0, 1]$ must cross the diagonal $y = x$ for some $x^* \in [0, 1]$. Hence, $f(x^*) = x^*$.
content.definition: A fixed point of a function $f: X \to X$ is a point $x \in X$ such that $f(x) = x$. The Brouwer fixed point theorem states that any continuous function from the n-dimensional disk to itself has at least one fixed point.
content.formal: $$f: \mathbb{D}^n \to \mathbb{D}^n \text{ continuous} \implies \exists x \in \mathbb{D}^n, f(x) = x.$$
content.intuition: Intuitively, if you have a continuous function that maps a disk to itself, you can imagine it as a deformation of the disk. No matter how you stretch or twist the disk, there will always be at least one point that ends up in the same place after the deformation.
content.gloss: You cannot continuously map a disc to itself without leaving some point fixed. In dimension $2$ the proof uses that no retraction $D^2\to S^1$ exists.

examples.tex: If $f$ had no fixed point, the ray from $f(x)$ through $x$ would define a retraction $D^2\to S^1$, contradicting $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

steps.content: Assume that $f$ has no fixed points. Then there is a retraction $r: D^2 \to S^{1}$ given by having $r(x)$ be the point on the ray that originates at $f (x)$ and passes through $x$ that lies on the circle. See Figure 8.3. $S^1 f (x) x r(x)$ Figure 8.3: The map $r: D^2 \to S^{1}$. By Lemma 7.30, the homomorphism $i*: \pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 ) \to \pi_1 (D^2, s_0 )$ induced by the inclusion map $i: S^{1} \to D^2$ must be a monomorphism. Since $D^2$ is simply connected (it has the homotopy type of a point), it follows by Theorem 8.16 that the homomorphism $i*$ cannot be a monomorphism. Hence, there is no retraction $r: D^2 \to S^{1}$.

content.statement: A simple closed curve in the plane separates the plane into an inside and an outside, with the curve as their common boundary.
content.formal: If $C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2$ is a simple closed curve, then $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus C$ has exactly two connected components, and $C$ is the boundary of each component.
content.intuition: Jordan curve theorem formalizes the planar fact that a circle-like curve cuts the plane into two regions.

concepts.label: Proof of Tychonoff via Alexander Subbase Theorem

content.statement: Proof outline: use the subbasis for the product topology, apply Alexander subbase theorem, and use choice/Zorn-style maximality to reduce a subbasic cover to finitely many coordinate conditions.
content.intuition: The proof packages the three deleted proof-step nodes: reduction to subbasic covers, choice of coordinates or maximal data, and the finite-subcover contradiction using compact factors.

concepts.label: Proof of Heine-Borel by Open Covers

content.intuition: The open-cover proof reduces compactness to the compact cube case and then passes compactness to closed subsets.

content.statement: $\mathbb{R}$ with its usual metric topology.
content.definition: R with its usual metric topology.

content.statement: $\mathbb{R}^n$ with its standard metric topology.
content.definition: R^n with its standard metric topology.

content.statement: A space in which every subset is open.
content.definition: A space in which every subset is open.

content.statement: A space with only $\emptyset$ and X open.
content.definition: A space with only ∅ and X open.

content.statement: The two-point space with open sets $\emptyset$, {1}, and {0,1}.
content.definition: The two-point space with open sets ∅, {1}, and {0,1}.

content.statement: The Cantor set $C\subseteq[0,1]$: remove the open middle third repeatedly; $C$ is what remains.
content.definition: The middle-thirds Cantor subset of [0,1].
content.intuition: A compact, totally disconnected, perfect set — uncountable yet measure zero.
content.gloss: Compact, uncountable, totally disconnected, and homeomorphic to $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb N}$.

examples.tex: $C$ is compact (closed and bounded in $\mathbb{R}$) and sequentially compact.

content.statement: $S^1$ with the subspace topology from $\mathbb{R}^2$.
content.definition: S^1 with the subspace topology from R^2.

content.statement: $S^1\times S^1$, equivalently a square with opposite sides identified.
content.definition: S^1×S^1, equivalently a square with opposite sides identified.

content.statement: $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology inherited from $\mathbb{R}$.
content.definition: Q with the subspace topology inherited from R.

content.statement: A non-second-countable ordered topological space built from uncountable ordinals.
content.definition: A non-second-countable ordered topological space built from uncountable ordinals.

content.statement: $\mathbb{R}$ with the lower-limit topology generated by [a,b).
content.definition: R with the lower-limit topology generated by [a,b).

content.statement: A union of countably many tangent circles with radii tending to zero.
content.definition: A union of countably many tangent circles with radii tending to zero.

content.statement: The closure of the graph of $\sin(1/x)$ for $x>0$.
content.definition: The closure of the graph of sin(1/x) for x>0.

content.statement: Spaces formed by identifying points, such as endpoints of an interval.
content.definition: Spaces formed by identifying points, such as endpoints of an interval.

content.statement: A bare set $X$ without a specified topology is not yet a topological space.
content.definition: A set considered without choosing which subsets are open.
content.intuition: Topology is extra structure; the same set can carry many different topologies.

content.statement: Sierpinski or indiscrete-type spaces showing failures after removing Hausdorff hypotheses.
content.definition: Sierpinski or indiscrete-type spaces showing failures after removing Hausdorff hypotheses.

content.statement: A compact space carrying a sequence with no convergent subsequence — witnessing that compactness does not imply sequential compactness.
content.definition: A compact space in which sequences do not detect compactness, such as certain large products.
content.intuition: Without first-countability, sequences are too weak to detect compactness; one needs nets or filters instead.
content.gloss: Compact but not sequentially compact — sequences fail to see the compactness.

examples.tex: $[0,1]^{[0,1]}$ (a product of $\mathfrak{c}$ copies of $[0,1]$) is compact by Tychonoff yet has a sequence with no convergent subsequence.

content.statement: A connected space such as the topologist's sine curve that is not path connected.
content.definition: A connected space such as the topologist's sine curve that is not path connected.

content.statement: Does d(𝑥, 𝑦) = (𝑥 − 𝑦)2 define a metric on X = ℝ?
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement: Show that ℝ2 equipped with the taxi cab metric is a metric space.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement:
Let (X, d) be a metric space. Show that the map d ′ ∶ X × X → ℝ given by
d(𝑥, 𝑦)
d ′ (𝑥, 𝑦) =
1 + d(𝑥, 𝑦)
is also a metric on X.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.
Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement: Show that d1 , d2 and d∞ (as defined in Exercise 2.4) are equivalent on X = ℝ2 .
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement:
Show that in a discrete metric space (X, d), cf. Example 2.3, every subset is both
open and closed in X.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement:
Show that for equivalent metrics d and d ′ on the set X, the open sets are the

content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement:
Let (X, dX ) and (𝑌 , d𝑌 ) be metric spaces, and let 𝑓 ∶ X → 𝑌 be a map. Show
that 𝑓 is continuous if and only if for every subset 𝐵 ⊆ 𝑌 closed in 𝑌 , the preimage 𝑓 −1 (𝐵) is
closed in X.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 2.

content.statement: Let $X = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Show that  $ \mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{b, c, d\}, X\}$ is not a topology on $X$. Find a topology $\mathcal{T}'$ (different from the discrete topology) on $X$ such that $\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}'$.

steps.content: To show that $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology, we need to check if it satisfies the \textit{three axioms of a topology}. $$\begin{aligned} \emptyset \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ and } X \in \mathcal{T} \\ \bigcup_{\alpha} U_\alpha \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ for any collection } \{U_\alpha\} \subseteq \mathcal{T} \\ \bigcap_{i=1}^n U_i \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ for any finite collection } U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n \in \mathcal{T} \end{aligned}$$

We can check that $\mathcal{T}$ satisfies T1 and T2, but it fails T3. For example, consider the sets $U_1 = \{a, b\}$ and $U_2 = \{b, c, d\}$, both of which are in $\mathcal{T}$. The intersection $U_1 \cap U_2 = \{b\}$ is not in $\mathcal{T}$, which violates T3. Therefore, $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology on $X$.

To find a topology $\mathcal{T}'$ such that $\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}'$, close the displayed family under the missing finite intersections. Since $\{a,b\}\cap\{b,c,d\}=\{b\}$ and $\{a,b,c\}\cap\{b,c,d\}=\{b,c\}$, define $$\mathcal{T}'=\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{b\},\{a,b\},\{b,c\},\{a,b,c\},\{b,c,d\},X\}.$$ This family contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, is closed under arbitrary unions, and is closed under finite intersections, so it is a topology on $X$ containing $\mathcal{T}$.
Thus $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology because it fails finite-intersection closure, while the enlarged family $\mathcal{T}'$ above is a non-discrete topology containing $\mathcal{T}$.

content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be non-empty, fix $x_0\in X$, and define $\mathcal{T}=\{U\subseteq X\mid x_0\notin U\text{ or }X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}$. ### Solution $\emptyset\in\mathcal{T}$ because it omits $x_0$, and $X\in\mathcal{T}$ because $X\setminus X=\emptyset$ is finite. For arbitrary unions, let $V=\bigcup U_\lambda$. If $x_0\notin V$, then $V\in\mathcal{T}$. If $x_0\in V$, then $x_0\in U_{\lambda_0}$ for some $\lambda_0$, so $X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite and $X\setminus V\subseteq X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite. For finite intersections $W=\bigcap_{i=1}^nU_i$, if some $U_i$ omits $x_0$, so does $W$; otherwise every $X\setminus U_i$ is finite and $X\setminus W=\bigcup_i(X\setminus U_i)$ is finite. Thus $\mathcal{T}$ is a topology.

content.statement:
Let X be a set, and let 𝐴 be a subset of X. Define the coarsest topology on X
such that 𝐴 is open in X.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be a set and $A\subseteq X$. Coarser means contained as a collection of open sets. ### Solution The coarsest topology on $X$ in which $A$ is open is $\mathcal{T}_A=\{\emptyset,A,X\}$. This is a topology because all unions and finite intersections of these three sets are again among these three sets. If $\mathcal{T}$ is any topology with $A\in\mathcal{T}$, then $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$ by the topology axioms, so $\mathcal{T}_A\subseteq\mathcal{T}$. Hence $\mathcal{T}_A$ is the desired coarsest topology.

content.statement:
Show that the discrete topology disc is finer than the cofinite topology cof on
any set X.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$ and $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}=\{U\subseteq X\mid X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}\cup\{\emptyset\}$. ### Solution Every $U\in\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}$ is a subset of $X$, and every subset of $X$ lies in $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Therefore $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$, so the discrete topology is finer. If $X$ is infinite this containment is strict because singletons are discrete-open but not cofinite-open; if $X$ is finite the two topologies agree.

content.statement:
Let X = {a, b, c, d}. Find two topologies 1 and 2 with 1 ≠ 2 such that a
bijection 𝑓 ∶ X → X is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given 1 as topology and the
codomain is given 2 as topology).
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X=\{a,b,c,d\}$. A homeomorphism is a bijection whose map and inverse are continuous. ### Solution Let $f$ swap $a$ and $b$ and fix $c,d$. Set $\mathcal{T}_1=\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{a,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$ and $\mathcal{T}_2=\{f(U)\mid U\in\mathcal{T}_1\}=\{\emptyset,\{b\},\{b,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$. Both are topologies by direct closure under unions and finite intersections. They are distinct since $\{a\}\in\mathcal{T}_1$ but $\{a\}\notin\mathcal{T}_2$. For each open set in $\mathcal{T}_2$, its preimage under $f$ is the corresponding open set in $\mathcal{T}_1$; since $f=f^{-1}$, the inverse is continuous by the same check. Thus the two spaces are homeomorphic with unequal topologies.

content.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of X.
(a) Assume that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵. Show that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵.
(b) Show that 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be a topological space and $A,B\subseteq X$. The closure $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$. ### Solution If $A\subseteq B$, every closed set containing $B$ also contains $A$, so $\overline{A}$ is contained in every closed superset of $B$ and hence $\overline{A}\subseteq\overline{B}$. For unions, the first part gives $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}\subseteq\overline{A\cup B}$. Conversely $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$ is closed as a finite union of closed sets and contains $A\cup B$, so minimality gives $\overline{A\cup B}\subseteq\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$. Therefore $\overline{A\cup B}=\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$.

content.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of X. Show that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 if
and only if every neighborhood of 𝑥 intersects 𝐴.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content:
### Setup and Definitions Let $A\subseteq X$. A neighbourhood of $x$ is an open set containing $x$, and $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$. ### Solution If some neighbourhood $U$ of $x$ satisfies $U\cap A=\emptyset$, then $A\subseteq X\setminus U$, where $X\setminus U$ is closed. Hence $\overline{A}\subseteq X\setminus U$, so $x
otin\overline{A}$. Conversely, if $x
otin\overline{A}$, then there is a closed set $C\supseteq A$ with $x
otin C$. Then $U=X\setminus C$ is an open neighbourhood of $x$ and $U\cap A=\emptyset$. Thus $x\in\overline{A}$ iff every neighbourhood of $x$ meets $A$.

content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.

steps.content: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology and $S=\{a,b\}$. A set is dense iff its closure is all of $X$. ### Solution Closed sets are complements of listed open sets. The closed sets containing $S$ are exactly $X$ and $\{a,b,c\}$, so $\overline{S}=X\cap\{a,b,c\}=\{a,b,c\}$. This is not $X$, so $S$ is not dense. Equivalently, the non-empty open set $\{d,e\}$ is disjoint from $S$.

content.statement:
Let X = {a, b, c, d, 𝑒}, and let
 = {∅, {a, b}, {a, b, d, 𝑒}, {b}, {b, d, 𝑒}, {b, c, d, 𝑒}, {c, d, 𝑒}, {d, 𝑒}, X}
be a topology on X. Show that  = {{a, b}, {b, d, 𝑒}, {c, d, 𝑒}} is a subbasis for  . Is  ′ =
{{a, b}, {b, c, d, 𝑒}, {d, 𝑒}} a subbasis for  ?
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology $\mathcal{T}$. ### Solution For $\mathcal{S}=\{\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\}\}$, the union is $X$. Its finite intersections give basis elements $\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\},\{b\},\{d,e\}$, whose unions are exactly the members of $\mathcal{T}$. Thus $\mathcal{S}$ is a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$. For $\mathcal{S}'=\{\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\}\}$, the generated basis is $\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\},\{b\}$. Unions of these sets cannot produce $\{c,d,e\}$, which is in $\mathcal{T}$, so $\mathcal{S}'$ is not a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$.

content.statement:
Let  be the collection of all open intervals (a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ a < 𝑥 < b} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
standard topology on ℝ denoted by std .
(b) Show that std = d where d is the metric topology obtained from the metric d(𝑥, 𝑦) =
|𝑥 − 𝑦|.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{R},a<b\}$. ### Solution For every $x$, $(x-1,x+1)$ contains $x$, so B1 holds. If $x\in(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, then $x\in(\max(a,c),\min(b,d))\subseteq(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, so B2 holds. Thus $\mathcal{B}$ is a basis. Metric balls for $|x-y|$ are intervals $(x-r,x+r)$, and every interval around a point contains such a ball by choosing $r=\min(x-a,b-x)$. Hence this basis generates the usual metric topology.

content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{S}=\{(a,\infty)\mid a\in\mathbb{R}\}\cup\{(-\infty,b)\mid b\in\mathbb{R}\}$. ### Solution The union of the subbasis elements is $\mathbb{R}$. Finite intersections are rays, empty sets, or intervals $(a,b)=(a,\infty)\cap(-\infty,b)$. Thus every standard basis interval is generated. Conversely, each ray is a union of standard open intervals, e.g. $(a,\infty)=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty(a,a+n)$. Therefore the topology generated by $\mathcal{S}$ is exactly the standard topology.

content.statement:
Let ℚ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let ℝ denote the set of real numbers.
Show that
 = {(a, b) ∣ a < b, a, b ∈ ℚ}
is a basis for the standard topology on ℝ where (a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ a < 𝑥 < b}.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}_{\mathbb{Q}}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{Q},a<b\}$. ### Solution Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives rational $a<x<b$ for each real $x$, so B1 holds. Intersections of rational-endpoint intervals are rational-endpoint intervals, so B2 holds. Every such interval is standard-open. Conversely, if $x\in(a,b)$ with real endpoints, choose rationals $p,q$ with $a<p<x<q<b$; then $(p,q)\subseteq(a,b)$. Hence these intervals generate the standard topology.

content.statement:
Let  be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣
a ⩽ 𝑥 < b} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
lower limit topology on ℝ.
(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of ℝ given the lower limit topology.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}_\ell=\{[a,b)\mid a<b\}$ and let $\mathcal{T}_\ell$ be the lower limit topology. ### Solution B1 follows from $x\in[x,x+1)$. B2 follows from $[a,b)\cap[c,d)=[\max(a,c),\min(b,d))$ when the intersection is non-empty. The closure of $(0,1)$ is $[0,1)$: first, $[0,1)$ is closed because its complement $(-\infty,0)\cup[1,\infty)$ is lower-limit-open. Thus the closure is contained in $[0,1)$. Every neighbourhood of $0$ contains some $[0,\varepsilon)$ meeting $(0,1)$, while $[1,2)$ is a neighbourhood of $1$ disjoint from $(0,1)$. Therefore $\overline{(0,1)}=[0,1)$.

content.statement:
For each 𝑛 ∈ ℤ, let
{
{𝑛} if 𝑛 is odd,
𝐵(𝑛) =
{𝑛 − 1, 𝑛, 𝑛 + 1} if 𝑛 is even.
Show that the collection  = {𝐵(𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ} is a basis for a topology on ℤ. The topology gen-
erated by  is known as the digital line topology on ℤ. See [1, pp. 44–46] for some applications
of this topology.
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup For $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, let $B(n)=\{n\}$ if $n$ is odd and $B(n)=\{n-1,n,n+1\}$ if $n$ is even. ### Solution Each $n$ lies in $B(n)$, so B1 holds. For B2, if an intersection contains an odd point $x$, then $B(x)=\{x\}$ refines the intersection. If it contains an even point $x$, then the only basis element with even center $x$ supplies the needed local refinement; neighbouring odd basis elements are singletons and cannot contain $x$. Hence every point in an intersection has a basis neighbourhood contained in that intersection, so the collection is a basis.

content.statement:
Let  be the collection of all subsets of the form 𝐴a,b = {a𝑧 + b ∣ 𝑧 ∈ ℤ} of ℤ,
where a, b ∈ ℤ and a ≠ 0. (The set 𝐴a,b is known as an arithmetic progression.)
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℤ.
(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by . (This
topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on ℤ and it was used originally
by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup For $a\ne0$, define $A_{a,b}=\{az+b\mid z\in\mathbb{Z}\}$. ### Solution B1 holds because $A_{1,0}=\mathbb{Z}$. If $n\in A_{a,b}\cap A_{c,d}$, then the intersection is the congruence class $A_{\operatorname{lcm}(|a|,|c|),n}$, so B2 holds. Each basis element is clopen because its complement is a finite union of the other residue classes modulo $|a|$. Every non-empty open set is infinite. If there were finitely many primes $p_i$, then $\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{-1,1\}=\bigcup_iA_{p_i,0}$ would be closed, so $\{-1,1\}$ would be open, contradicting that no non-empty finite set is open. Therefore there are infinitely many primes.

content.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on X. Show
that a subset 𝐴 of X is dense in X if and only if every non-empty basis element in  intersects
𝐴. (Recall that a set 𝑈 intersects a set 𝑉 if 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ≠ ∅.)
content.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.

steps.content: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for $X$ and $A\subseteq X$. ### Solution If $A$ is dense, every non-empty open set meets $A$, hence every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Conversely, assume every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Given a non-empty open set $U$, choose $x\in U$ and a basis element $B$ with $x\in B\subseteq U$. Then $B\cap A\ne\emptyset$, so $U\cap A\ne\emptyset$. Thus every non-empty open set meets $A$, and $A$ is dense.

where ℝP𝑛 is real projective 𝑛-space. (You may assume as a known fact that the 𝑛-sphere 𝑆 𝑛
