content.statement: A \textbf{based map} $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ is a continuous map $h\colon X\to Y$ with $h(x_0)=y_0$.
content.intuition: A continuous map that respects chosen basepoints. Based maps are what induce homomorphisms of fundamental groups.
content.gloss: A continuous map that respects chosen basepoints. Based maps are what induce homomorphisms of fundamental groups.

examples.tex: Composing a loop at $x_0$ with $h$ gives a loop at $y_0$.

content.statement: If $X$ is path-connected and $x_0,x_1\in X$, then $\pi_1(X,x_0)\cong\pi_1(X,x_1)$.
content.intuition: In a path-connected space the fundamental group does not depend (up to isomorphism) on the chosen basepoint — so one often writes $\pi_1(X)$.
content.gloss: In a path-connected space the fundamental group does not depend (up to isomorphism) on the chosen basepoint — so one often writes $\pi_1(X)$.

examples.tex: A path $\alpha$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ induces the isomorphism $\hat\alpha([f])=[\alpha]^{-1}*[f]*[\alpha]$.

steps.content: Let $\alpha: I \to X$ be a path from $x_0$ to $x_1$. If $f: I \to X$ is a loop in $X$ based $x_0$, then $\alpha * f * \alpha: \hat{\pi}_1 (X, x_0 ) \to \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$ by is a loop in $X$ based at $x_1$. We define the map $\alpha ([\hat{f} ]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha] \alpha$ where we have used the fact that [ $\alpha ] = [\alpha]^{-1}$. It is a well-defined map as $*$ is well-defined, and moreover, it only depends on the path homotopy class of $\alpha$. We will show that $\alpha: \hat{\pi}_1 (X, x_0 ) \to \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$ is an isomorphism. We will first show that $\alpha$ is a homomorphism, and then show that it is bijective. Since $([\hat{f} ] * [g]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [g] * [\alpha] \alpha = ([\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha]) * ([\alpha]^{-1} * [g] * [\alpha]) =\alpha ([\hat{f} ]) * \alpha ([\hat{g}])$, for all $[f ], [g] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$, it follows that $\alpha$ is a homomorphism. To show that $\alpha$ is bijective, and hence, an isomorphism, we show that $\alpha$ has an inverse. Let $\beta = \alpha$, i.e., $\beta$ is the reverse path of $\alpha$. Since $\hat{\beta}([h]) = [\beta]^{-1} * [h] * [\beta] = [\alpha] * [h] * [\alpha]^{-1} \beta([h])) = [\alpha]^{-1} * ([\beta]^{-1} * [h] * [\beta]) * [\alpha] = [h] ( \hat{\alpha}$ for all $[h] \in \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$, and $([\hat{f} ]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha] = [\beta] * [f ] * [\beta]^{-1} \alpha \hat{\alpha} ([f ])) = [\beta]^{-1} * ([\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha]) * [\beta] = [f ] \beta( \hat{\alpha} )^{-1}$. for all $[f ] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$, it follows that $\hat{\beta} = ($

content.statement: A \textbf{binary operation} on a set $S$ is a map $*\colon S\times S\to S$; one writes $a*b$ for $*(a,b)$.
content.intuition: A rule combining two elements of $S$ into a third, with $S$ closed under it. The raw ingredient of a group.
content.gloss: A rule combining two elements of $S$ into a third, with $S$ closed under it. The raw ingredient of a group.

examples.tex: Addition and multiplication on $\mathbb{Z}$ are binary operations.

content.statement: In a metric space $(X,d)$, a subset $A$ is \textbf{bounded} if there is $M\in\mathbb{R}$ with $d(a_1,a_2)\le M$ for all $a_1,a_2\in A$.
content.intuition: The set fits inside some ball of finite radius — its points are never more than $M$ apart.
content.gloss: The set fits inside some ball of finite radius — its points are never more than $M$ apart.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}^n$, $A$ is bounded iff it sits inside some ball about the origin. Boundedness is metric, not topological.

content.statement: $A\times B=\{(a,b)\mid a\in A,\ b\in B\}$, the set of ordered pairs with first coordinate in $A$ and second in $B$.
content.definition: A construction combining two sets into the set of all ordered pairs.
content.formal: $A\times B=\{(a,b):a\in A\land b\in B\}$.
content.intuition: The set underlying product spaces and the ambient set for binary relations.
content.gloss: All pairings of an element of $A$ with an element of $B$ — the set underlying the product topology.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^2=\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$; the torus is $S^1\times S^1$.

content.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{cofinite topology} consists of $\varnothing$ together with all $U\subseteq X$ whose complement $X\setminus U$ is finite.
content.intuition: A subset is open exactly when it misses only finitely many points. A natural example of a topology that need not come from a metric.
content.gloss: A subset is open exactly when it misses only finitely many points. A natural example of a topology that need not come from a metric.

examples.tex: If $X$ is finite, the cofinite topology equals the discrete topology; if $X$ is infinite it is strictly coarser.

content.statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{compact} if it is compact in the subspace topology; equivalently, every cover of $A$ by open subsets of $X$ has a finite subcollection covering $A$ (Lemma 6.34).
content.definition: A subset A of a topological space X is compact if A is compact with the subspace topology. Equivalently, every cover of A by open subsets of X has a finite subcover of A.
content.formal: $$A\subseteq X\text{ is compact}\Longleftrightarrow (A,\tau_A)\text{ is compact},\quad \tau_A=\{A\cap U:U\in\tau_X\}.$$
content.intuition: Compactness of a subset can be tested with open sets of the ambient space — often more convenient.
content.gloss: Compactness of a subset can be tested with open sets of the ambient space — often more convenient.

examples.tex: $S^n\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ is compact; the torus $T^2$ is compact.

content.statement: For topologies $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ on $X$: if $\mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2$ we say $\mathcal{T}_1$ is \textbf{coarser} and $\mathcal{T}_2$ is \textbf{finer}. They are \textbf{comparable} if one contains the other.
content.intuition: Topologies on a fixed set are partially ordered by inclusion. “Finer” means more open sets (more resolving power); “coarser” means fewer.
content.gloss: Topologies on a fixed set are partially ordered by inclusion. “Finer” means more open sets (more resolving power); “coarser” means fewer.

examples.tex: $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ for every $\mathcal{T}$. Not every pair of topologies is comparable.

content.statement: For $B\subseteq X$, the complement of $B$ in $X$ is $X\setminus B$.
content.definition: The part of the ambient set outside a given subset.
content.formal: $B^c=X\setminus B=\{x\in X\mid x\notin B\}$.
content.intuition: The operation linking open and closed sets.
content.gloss: The difference of $A$ and $B$ when an ambient set $A$ is understood. Openness and closedness in topology are dual via complements.

examples.tex: In a topological space, $K$ is closed iff $K^c$ is open.

content.statement: Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be topological spaces. Then homeomorphism satisfies $$\begin{aligned} \textbf{Reflexivity:} \quad &X \cong X, \\ \textbf{Symmetry:} \quad &X \cong Y \Longrightarrow Y \cong X, \\ \textbf{Transitivity:} \quad &X \cong Y \text{ and } Y \cong Z \Longrightarrow X \cong Z. \end{aligned}$$ Equivalently, the relation $\cong$ of being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on topological spaces.
content.intuition: Homeomorphism is topology's notion of sameness of shape. Reflexivity says every space has the same shape as itself. Symmetry says a homeomorphism can be reversed. Transitivity says two reversible deformations can be chained.
content.gloss: Homeomorphism is topology's notion of sameness of shape. Reflexivity says every space has the same shape as itself. Symmetry says a homeomorphism can be reversed. Transitivity says two reversible deformations can be chained.

examples.tex: The identity map $\operatorname{id}_X \colon X \to X$ is a homeomorphism, so $X \cong X$. If $f \colon X \to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $f^{-1} \colon Y \to X$ is also a homeomorphism. If $f \colon X \to Y$ and $g \colon Y \to Z$ are homeomorphisms, then $$\begin{aligned} g \circ f \colon X \to Z \end{aligned}$$ is a homeomorphism with inverse $$\begin{aligned} (g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}. \end{aligned}$$

steps.content: The identity map $\operatorname{id}_X:X\to X$ is continuous, bijective, and its own inverse. Hence $\operatorname{id}_X$ is a homeomorphism and $X\cong X$.
If $f:X\to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $f^{-1}:Y\to X$ is continuous and bijective with inverse $f$. Hence $X\cong Y$ implies $Y\cong X$.

content.statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ are continuous, then $g\circ f\colon X\to Z$ is continuous.
content.intuition: Continuity is preserved under composition — the property that makes topological spaces and continuous maps into a category.
content.gloss: Continuity is preserved under composition — the property that makes topological spaces and continuous maps into a category.

examples.tex: Used repeatedly, e.g. to show that being homeomorphic is transitive (Thm. 3.23).

steps.content: Let $W\subseteq Z$ be open. To prove $g\circ f$ is continuous, it is enough to show that $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)$ is open in $X$.
The preimage satisfies $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)=f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$.
Since $g$ is continuous, $g^{-1}(W)$ is open in $Y$; since $f$ is continuous, $f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ is open in $X$. Hence $g\circ f$ is continuous.

content.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to Z$, the composition $g\circ f:X\to Z$ is defined by $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$.
content.definition: The operation of applying one function after another.
content.formal: $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$ whenever $\operatorname{cod}(f)=\operatorname{dom}(g)$.
content.intuition: Do $f$ first, then $g$. This is the backbone of continuity and functoriality.
content.gloss: Apply $f$, then $g$. Composition is associative and the identity acts as a unit — the backbone of categorical thinking in topology.

examples.tex: $\mathrm{id}_B\circ f=f=f\circ\mathrm{id}_A$; continuity and homomorphism properties are preserved under composition.

content.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous at $p$ iff for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there is a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ with $f(A)\subseteq B$.
content.intuition: Reformulates $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity purely with neighborhoods — the bridge that lets continuity make sense without a metric.
content.gloss: Reformulates $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity purely with neighborhoods — the bridge that lets continuity make sense without a metric.

examples.tex: Recovers the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition by taking $B=\mathsf{B}_Y(f(p);\epsilon)$ and $A=\mathsf{B}_X(p;\delta)$.

steps.content: Assume $f$ is continuous at $p$. Given a neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$, choose an open ball around $f(p)$ inside $B$, then use the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition to find a neighborhood of $p$ mapped into $B$.
Conversely, take $B=\mathsf{B}_Y(f(p);\varepsilon)$. The neighborhood condition gives a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ mapped into $B$, and an open ball around $p$ inside $A$ supplies $\delta$.
The neighborhood formulation and the metric $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ formulation of continuity at $p$ are equivalent.

content.statement: Let $X$ and $Y$ be topological spaces, let $x\in X$, and let $f\colon X\to Y$. The map $f$ is \textbf{continuous at} $x$ if for every neighborhood $V$ of $f(x)$ there is a neighborhood $U$ of $x$ such that $f(U)\subseteq V$. $$\begin{aligned} f\text{ is continuous at }x \quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad \forall V\in\mathcal{N}_Y(f(x))\;\exists U\in\mathcal{N}_X(x)\text{ such that } f(U)\subseteq V. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Local continuity stated with neighborhoods. A map is continuous iff it is continuous at every point (Thm. 3.18).
content.gloss: Local continuity stated with neighborhoods. A map is continuous iff it is continuous at every point (Thm. 3.18).

examples.tex: Directly generalises the metric neighborhood criterion of Thm. 2.12.

content.statement: If $\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $\mathcal{S}$) is a basis (resp. subbasis) for $Y$, then $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(B)$ is open for every $B\in\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $f^{-1}(S)$ open for every $S\in\mathcal{S}$).
content.intuition: To check continuity you need only test preimages of basis (or subbasis) elements, not all open sets — a big practical economy.
content.gloss: To check continuity you need only test preimages of basis (or subbasis) elements, not all open sets — a big practical economy.

examples.tex: Crucial for proving that projections out of a product are continuous.

steps.content: If $f$ is continuous and $B\in\mathcal{B}$ is a basis element of $Y$, then $B$ is open and $f^{-1}(B)$ is open in $X$.
Conversely, every open $V\subseteq Y$ is a union of basis elements. Since preimages commute with unions, $f^{-1}(V)$ is a union of open sets when every basis-element preimage is open.
The same reduction applies to subbases by first passing from subbasis elements to finite intersections and then to arbitrary unions.

concepts.label: Continuity via Preimages of Open Sets

content.statement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces, and let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a function. Then $f$ is continuous if and only if, for every open set $V\subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)=\{x\in X: f(x)\in V\}$ is open in $X$. $$ f\colon X\to Y \text{ is continuous} \iff \forall V\subseteq Y,\ \bigl(V \text{ is open in } Y \Rightarrow f^{-1}(V) \text{ is open in } X\bigr). $$
content.intuition: Continuity can be tested entirely by pulling back open sets: open sets in the codomain must have open preimages in the domain. This reformulates the usual distance-based idea of continuity in a way that no longer explicitly mentions distances, which is why it becomes the standard definition in topology.
content.gloss: Continuity can be tested entirely by pulling back open sets: open sets in the codomain must have open preimages in the domain. This reformulates the usual distance-based idea of continuity in a way that no longer explicitly mentions distances, which is why it becomes the standard definition in topology.

examples.tex: This open-set criterion becomes the definition of continuity between topological spaces.

steps.content: Assume $f$ is continuous and $B\subseteq Y$ is open. For $a\in f^{-1}(B)$, choose an open ball around $f(a)$ inside $B$ and then use continuity to find a ball around $a$ mapped into it.
Conversely, if every open $B\subseteq Y$ has open preimage, apply this to $\mathsf{B}_Y(f(a);\varepsilon)$ and use openness of its preimage to get a suitable $\delta$.
The proof also uses basic preimage rules: preimages commute with unions, intersections, complements, and preserve inclusions. These rules do not require an inverse map.

concepts.label: Continuous Image of a Compact Space

content.statement: If $X$ is compact and $f:X\to Y$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ is compact; if $f$ is also surjective, then $Y$ is compact.
content.formal: If $f:X\to Y$ is continuous and $X$ is compact, then $f(X)$ is compact. In particular, if $f$ is surjective, then $Y$ is compact.
content.intuition: Continuity preserves compactness, just as it preserves connectedness. A key step toward the extreme value theorem.
content.gloss: Continuity preserves compactness, just as it preserves connectedness. A key step toward the extreme value theorem.

examples.tex: Pull an open cover of $Y$ back through $f$, take a finite subcover upstairs, push forward.

steps.content: Let $\mathcal{C}=\{U_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ be an open cover of $Y$. The family $\{f^{-1}(U_\lambda)\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ is an open cover of $X$ by continuity.
Since $X$ is compact, finitely many pulled-back sets $f^{-1}(U_{\lambda_1}),\ldots,f^{-1}(U_{\lambda_n})$ cover $X$.
The corresponding sets $U_{\lambda_1},\ldots,U_{\lambda_n}$ cover $Y$, so the original cover has a finite subcover. Thus $Y$ is compact.

concepts.label: Continuous Image of a Connected Space

content.statement: If $X$ is connected, $Y$ a space, and $f\colon X\to Y$ a surjective continuous map, then $Y$ is connected. Hence $f(X)$ is connected for any continuous $f$.
content.intuition: Continuity cannot tear a connected space apart: its image stays in one piece. This makes connectedness a topological invariant.
content.gloss: Continuity cannot tear a connected space apart: its image stays in one piece. This makes connectedness a topological invariant.

examples.tex: Yields the Intermediate Value Theorem as a special case (Thm. 6.13).

steps.content: Prove the equivalent statement: if $f:X\to Y$ is surjective and continuous and $Y$ is disconnected, then $X$ is disconnected.
Let $U,V$ be a separation of $Y$. Then $f^{-1}(U)$ and $f^{-1}(V)$ are nonempty open subsets of $X$ because $f$ is surjective and continuous.
The preimages are disjoint and their union is $X$, so they form a separation of $X$. Hence connectedness of $X$ forces connectedness of $Y$.

content.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ between metric spaces is \textbf{continuous at} $p$ if $\forall\,\epsilon>0\ \exists\,\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta \Rightarrow d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. It is \textbf{continuous} if it is continuous at every $p\in X$.
content.intuition: The familiar $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition: points close in the domain are sent to points close in the codomain. Nearness is measured by the two metrics.
content.gloss: The familiar $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition: points close in the domain are sent to points close in the codomain. Nearness is measured by the two metrics.

examples.tex: Every map out of a discrete metric space is continuous; the identity $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, but $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is not.

content.statement: A collection $\mathcal{A}$ of subsets is a \textbf{cover} of $X$ if $X=\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}}A$. It is an \textbf{open cover} if each $A$ is open. $\mathcal{A}'\subseteq\mathcal{A}$ is a \textbf{subcover} if it still covers.
content.intuition: A way of overlaying $X$ with (open) patches. The notion of “every open cover has a finite subcover” defines compactness.
content.gloss: A way of overlaying $X$ with (open) patches. The notion of “every open cover has a finite subcover” defines compactness.

examples.tex: Any basis or subbasis for $X$ is an open cover of $X$.

content.statement: A surjective continuous $p\colon E\to B$ is a \textbf{covering map} if every $b\in B$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $p^{-1}(U)=\bigsqcup_{\lambda}V_\lambda$ a disjoint union of opens, each mapped homeomorphically onto $U$ by $p$. $E$ \textbf{covers} $B$.
content.intuition: $B$ is “evenly covered”: locally $E$ looks like a stack of disjoint copies of a patch of $B$. The route to computing $\pi_1$ via lifting.
content.gloss: $B$ is “evenly covered”: locally $E$ looks like a stack of disjoint copies of a patch of $B$. The route to computing $\pi_1$ via lifting.

examples.tex: $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $t\mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is the universal cover of the circle.

content.statement: For $x\in G$ (multiplicative), $\langle x\rangle=\{x^n\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a subgroup — the \textbf{cyclic subgroup generated by} $x$, the smallest subgroup containing $x$. $G$ is \textbf{cyclic} if $G=\langle x'\rangle$ for some $x'$.
content.intuition: The powers of a single element form a subgroup. A whole group generated this way is cyclic; all cyclic groups are abelian.
content.gloss: The powers of a single element form a subgroup. A whole group generated this way is cyclic; all cyclic groups are abelian.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{Z}=\langle 1\rangle=\langle -1\rangle$ is infinite cyclic. $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is cyclic.

steps.content: Since $H$ is a subgroup of $G$, it follows that $x^{-1} \in H$. Thus $x_{x-1} = x^{-1} x = x_0 = 1 \in H$. Furthermore, $x_n \in H$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ where $x-m = (x^{-1} )m$. Thus $\langle x\rangle$ is a subgroup of $H$.

content.statement: A homotopy $H\colon X\times I\to X$ is a \textbf{deformation retraction} onto $A$ if $H(x,0)=x$, $H(x,1)\in A$ for all $x$, and $H(a,t)=a$ for all $a\in A$, $t\in I$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{deformation retract}.
content.intuition: Continuously shrink $X$ onto $A$ over time, holding $A$ fixed throughout. It exhibits $X$ and $A$ as homotopy equivalent.
content.gloss: Continuously shrink $X$ onto $A$ over time, holding $A$ fixed throughout. It exhibits $X$ and $A$ as homotopy equivalent.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ deformation retracts onto $S^1$; a disc retracts onto its centre.

content.statement: The difference $A\setminus B$ is the set of elements of $A$ that are not elements of $B$.
content.definition: The operation of removing one set from another.
content.formal: $A\setminus B=\{x\in A\mid x\notin B\}$.
content.intuition: What remains of $A$ after excluding $B$. Complements are relative set differences.
content.gloss: What remains of $A$ after removing $B$. Disjoint from $B$, with $(A\setminus B)\cup B=A\cup B$.

content.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{discrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$, the power set: \textbf{every} subset is open.
content.intuition: The finest possible topology — a space of isolated points with no interaction. Singletons are open here, which uniquely characterises it.
content.gloss: The finest possible topology — a space of isolated points with no interaction. Singletons are open here, which uniquely characterises it.

examples.tex: For any set $X$, $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is the largest topology; every map out of a discrete space is continuous.

content.statement: For a family $(A_i)_{i\in I}$, the disjoint union is $\bigsqcup_{i\in I}A_i=\bigcup_{i\in I}(A_i\times\{i\})$.
content.definition: A union whose elements are tagged by their index so pieces remain distinct.
content.formal: $\bigsqcup_{i\in I}A_i=\{(a,i)\mid i\in I,\ a\in A_i\}$.
content.intuition: Copies of the same point from different pieces do not accidentally merge.
content.gloss: Combine sets while keeping track of which piece each element came from, so copies never merge. Used to describe $p^{-1}(U)$ for covering maps.

examples.tex: The preimage of an evenly covered set is a disjoint union of homeomorphic sheets (Def. 8.1).

content.statement: For an equivalence relation $\sim$ on $X$, the \textbf{equivalence class} of $x$ is $[x]=\{y\in X\mid x\sim y\}$, and $X/\!\sim\ =\{[x]\mid x\in X\}$.
content.intuition: Classes partition $X$: any two are equal or disjoint, and together they cover $X$. The quotient set $X/\!\sim$ collects them.
content.gloss: Classes partition $X$: any two are equal or disjoint, and together they cover $X$. The quotient set $X/\!\sim$ collects them.

examples.tex: Equivalence relations correspond (up to bijection) to surjective maps $X\to A$ (Lemma 5.15).

content.statement: For based maps $h_1\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ and $h_2\colon(Y,y_0)\to(Z,z_0)$: $(h_2\circ h_1)_*=(h_2)_*\circ(h_1)_*$, and $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*=\mathrm{id}$.
content.intuition: $\pi_1$ is a functor: it sends spaces to groups and maps to homomorphisms, respecting identities and composition.
content.gloss: $\pi_1$ is a functor: it sends spaces to groups and maps to homomorphisms, respecting identities and composition.

examples.tex: Immediately gives that homeomorphic based spaces have isomorphic $\pi_1$ (Cor. 7.25).

steps.content: For a loop $f$ based at $x_0$, $(h_2\circ h_1)_*([f])=[(h_2\circ h_1)\circ f]$.
Associativity gives $(h_2\circ h_1)\circ f=h_2\circ(h_1\circ f)$, so $(h_2\circ h_1)_*=h_{2*}\circ h_{1*}$.
For the identity map, $(\operatorname{id}_X)_*([f])=[\operatorname{id}_X\circ f]=[f]$, so the induced map is the identity automorphism of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$.

content.statement: For a based space $(X,x_0)$, a \textbf{loop} at $x_0$ is a path from $x_0$ to $x_0$. The \textbf{fundamental group} is $\pi_1(X,x_0)=\{[f]\mid f\ \text{a loop at}\ x_0\}$ with operation $*$.
content.intuition: The group of loops at $x_0$ up to path homotopy. The identity is the constant loop and inverses are reversed loops. It measures “holes” the space cannot retract away.
content.gloss: The group of loops at $x_0$ up to path homotopy. The identity is the constant loop and inverses are reversed loops. It measures “holes” the space cannot retract away.

examples.tex: $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^n,0)=0$ (trivial); $\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (Thm. 8.16).

content.statement: For a homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$, the map $\bar\varphi\colon G_1/\ker\varphi\to\operatorname{im}\varphi$, $\bar\varphi(x\ker\varphi)=\varphi(x)$, is an isomorphism, and $\varphi=\bar\varphi\circ\pi$ where $\pi\colon G_1\to G_1/\ker\varphi$.
content.intuition: Any homomorphism factors as a quotient followed by an isomorphism: $G_1/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. The first isomorphism theorem.
content.gloss: Any homomorphism factors as a quotient followed by an isomorphism: $G_1/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. The first isomorphism theorem.

examples.tex: Identifies the image of $\varphi$ with the quotient of $G_1$ by what $\varphi$ collapses.

steps.content: We first show that $\phi$ is well-defined. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be two elements in $G$. If $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$, we must show that $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. In other words, we must show that $\phi(x_1 ) = \phi(x_2 )$. Since $x_2 \in x_1$ ker $\phi$, there is an element $z$ in ker $\phi$ such that $x_2 = x_1 z$. Thus if $e_2$ is the identity element in $G_2$, we have $\phi(x_2 ) = \phi(x_1 z) = \phi(x_1 )\phi(z) = \phi(x_1 )e_2 = \phi(x_1 )$ where we have used the fact that $\phi$ is a homomorphism. Hence, $\phi$ is a well-defined map. We now show that $\phi$ is an isomorphism, where we first show that it is a homomorphism, and then that it is bijective. By definition for $x_1$ ker $\phi, x_2$ ker $\phi \in G/$ ker $\phi$, we have $\phi((x_1$ ker $\phi)(x_2$ ker $\phi)) = \phi((x_1 x_2 )$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_1 x_2 ) = \phi(x_1 )\phi(x_2 ) = \phi(x_1$ ker $\phi)\phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. Thus $\phi$ is a homomorphism. Now assume that $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. We must show that this implies that $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$. If $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$, then by definition, we have $\phi(x_1 ) = \phi(x_2 )$. This implies that $\phi(x_2 )^{-1} \phi(x_1 ) = e_2$. Since $\phi$ is a homomorphism, we have $\phi(x^{-1} 2 x_1 ) = e_2$. Thus $x^{-1} x_1 \in$ ker $\phi$, and $-1$ so, $x_2 x_1$ ker $\phi =$ ker $\phi$. Hence, $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$. Thus $\phi$ is injective. For any $\phi(x) \in$ im $\phi$, we have, by definition, that $\phi(x$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x)$. Hence, $\phi$ is surjective. Thus $\phi$ is bijective, and hence, it is an isomorphism. By definition, we have, for elements $x_1$ and $x_2$ in $G_1$, that $\pi(x_1 x_2 ) = (x_1 x_2 )$ ker $\phi = (x_1$ ker $\phi)(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. Appendix B. Elementary algebra 109 Hence, $\pi$ is a homomorphism. Furthermore, $(\phi\circ\pi)(x) = \phi(\pi(x)) = \phi(x$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x)$ for each $x \in G_1$. This completes the proof.

content.statement: Every polynomial $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_0$ of degree $n>0$ with complex coefficients has a complex root.
content.intuition: Complex polynomials always have roots — proved topologically via winding numbers of loops, i.e. via $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.
content.gloss: Complex polynomials always have roots — proved topologically via winding numbers of loops, i.e. via $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

examples.tex: A non-vanishing polynomial would let a large loop be contracted, contradicting that its image winds $n$ times.

content.statement: If $X$ is compact and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then there exist $m,M\in X$ with $f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)$ for all $x\in X$.
content.formal: If $X$ is compact and $f:X\to\mathbb R$ is continuous, then there exist $m,M\in X$ such that $f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)$ for all $x\in X$.
content.intuition: A continuous real function on a compact space attains its maximum and minimum. The image is compact, hence closed and bounded, hence contains its sup and inf.
content.gloss: A continuous real function on a compact space attains its maximum and minimum. The image is compact, hence closed and bounded, hence contains its sup and inf.

examples.tex: The classical “continuous functions on $[a,b]$ attain extrema” is the case $X=[a,b]$.

steps.content: By compactness preservation, $f(X)$ is compact in $\mathbb{R}$.
By Heine-Borel in $\mathbb{R}$, $f(X)$ is closed and bounded, so it has a supremum and an infimum.
If $s=\sup f(X)$ were not in $f(X)$, closedness of $f(X)$ would give a neighborhood of $s$ disjoint from $f(X)$, producing a smaller upper bound and a contradiction.
The infimum is handled similarly. Thus $f$ attains both a minimum and a maximum on $X$.

concepts.label: Supremum (Least Upper Bound)

content.statement: For $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, a number $s$ is the \textbf{supremum} of $A$ if $s$ is an upper bound for $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s\leq u$.
content.definition: The least upper bound of a subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
content.formal: $$s=\sup A\Longleftrightarrow (\forall a\in A,\ a\leq s)\text{ and }(\forall u,\ (\forall a\in A,\ a\leq u)\Rightarrow s\leq u).$$
content.intuition: The supremum is the smallest ceiling above a set. In compactness arguments, closedness can force this ceiling to lie inside the set, turning a bound into an attained maximum.

assumptions: $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is nonempty and bounded above when existence is asserted.

content.statement: Let $X$ be connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. If $a,b\in X$ and $r$ lies between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then $f(c)=r$ for some $c\in X$.
content.intuition: A continuous real-valued function on a connected space hits every intermediate value — the classical IVT, freed from intervals.
content.gloss: A continuous real-valued function on a connected space hits every intermediate value — the classical IVT, freed from intervals.

examples.tex: If $r\notin f(X)$ then $f^{-1}((-\infty,r))$ and $f^{-1}((r,\infty))$ would separate $X$.

steps.content: Assume $r\notin f(X)$. Then $f(X)\subseteq (-\infty,r)\cup(r,\infty)$.
Set $U=f^{-1}((-\infty,r))$ and $V=f^{-1}((r,\infty))$. These are disjoint nonempty open sets whose union is $X$.
The pair $U,V$ separates $X$, contradicting connectedness. Hence $r\in f(X)$, so $f(c)=r$ for some $c\in X$.

content.statement: A \textbf{group} is a non-empty set $G$ with a binary operation $*$ such that:\;\textbf{(G1)}\ associativity $(a*b)*c=a*(b*c)$;\;\textbf{(G2)}\ an identity $e$ with $a*e=a=e*a$;\;\textbf{(G3)}\ inverses $a'$ with $a*a'=e=a'*a$. It is \textbf{abelian} if $*$ is commutative.
content.intuition: A set with an associative, invertible operation and a neutral element. The algebraic target of the fundamental group functor.
content.gloss: A set with an associative, invertible operation and a neutral element. The algebraic target of the fundamental group functor.

examples.tex: $(\mathbb{Z},+)$ is an abelian group; $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is a group under $*$ (Thm. 7.16).

content.statement: A map $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$ is a \textbf{homomorphism} if $\varphi(x*_1 y)=\varphi(x)*_2\varphi(y)$ for all $x,y$.
content.intuition: A map respecting the group operations. Automatically $\varphi(e_1)=e_2$ and $\varphi(x^{-1})=\varphi(x)^{-1}$. Composites of homomorphisms are homomorphisms.
content.gloss: A map respecting the group operations. Automatically $\varphi(e_1)=e_2$ and $\varphi(x^{-1})=\varphi(x)^{-1}$. Composites of homomorphisms are homomorphisms.

examples.tex: The induced map $h_*\colon\pi_1(X)\to\pi_1(Y)$ is a homomorphism (Def. 7.22).

content.statement: For a continuous bijection $f\colon X\to Y$, the following are equivalent: (1) $f$ is a homeomorphism; (2) $f$ is an open map; (3) $f$ is a closed map.
content.intuition: For a continuous bijection, having a continuous inverse is the same as being open, which is the same as being closed.
content.gloss: For a continuous bijection, having a continuous inverse is the same as being open, which is the same as being closed.

examples.tex: Often the quickest route to proving a continuous bijection is a homeomorphism (e.g. from a compact space to a Hausdorff one).

steps.content: If $f$ is a homeomorphism and $U\subseteq X$ is open, then $f(U)=(f^{-1})^{-1}(U)$ is open in $Y$ because $f^{-1}$ is continuous.
If $f$ is a bijective open continuous map, then for every open $U\subseteq X$, $f(U)$ is open, which says $f^{-1}:Y\to X$ is continuous. Hence $f$ is a homeomorphism.
For a bijection, $f(X\setminus U)=Y\setminus f(U)$. Therefore preserving open sets is equivalent to preserving closed sets.

content.statement: Continuous maps $f_0,f_1\colon X\to Y$ are \textbf{homotopic} ($f_0\simeq f_1$) if there is a continuous $H\colon X\times I\to Y$ with $H(x,0)=f_0(x)$ and $H(x,1)=f_1(x)$. $H$ is a \textbf{homotopy}.
content.intuition: A continuous one-parameter deformation of one map into another. Think of $t\in[0,1]$ as time, sliding $f_0$ to $f_1$.
content.gloss: A continuous one-parameter deformation of one map into another. Think of $t\in[0,1]$ as time, sliding $f_0$ to $f_1$.

examples.tex: If $f_0\simeq f_1$ with $f_1$ constant, $f_0$ is \emph{nullhomotopic}. Any two maps into $\mathbb{R}^n$ are homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

content.statement: On $C(X,Y)$ (continuous maps $X\to Y$), $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation. Its classes are \textbf{homotopy classes}; $[f]$ denotes the class of $f$ and $[X,Y]=C(X,Y)/\!\simeq$.
content.intuition: Bundle maps that can be deformed into one another. Topology often studies $[X,Y]$ rather than individual maps.
content.gloss: Bundle maps that can be deformed into one another. Topology often studies $[X,Y]$ rather than individual maps.

examples.tex: $[X,\mathbb{R}^n]$ has a single element, since all such maps are homotopic.

content.statement: Maps $f\colon X\to Y$, $g\colon Y\to X$ are \textbf{homotopy equivalences} if $g\circ f\simeq\mathrm{id}_X$ and $f\circ g\simeq\mathrm{id}_Y$; each is a \textbf{homotopy inverse} of the other.
content.intuition: A weaker sameness than homeomorphism: the spaces are interchangeable up to deformation. Every homeomorphism is a homotopy equivalence, but not conversely.
content.gloss: A weaker sameness than homeomorphism: the spaces are interchangeable up to deformation. Every homeomorphism is a homotopy equivalence, but not conversely.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}$ and a point $\{p\}$ are homotopy equivalent but not homeomorphic.

content.statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ is a homotopy equivalence with $f(x_0)=y_0$, then $f_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
content.intuition: $\pi_1$ is an invariant of homotopy type, not merely of homeomorphism type — a stronger and very useful fact.
content.gloss: $\pi_1$ is an invariant of homotopy type, not merely of homeomorphism type — a stronger and very useful fact.

examples.tex: Lets you compute $\pi_1$ of a space via any simpler space it deformation retracts onto.

steps.content: Let $g: Y \to X$ be a homotopy inverse of $f$ with $g(y_0 ) = x_1$, and let $f (x_1 ) = y_1$. By assumption, we have $g\circ f \simeq$ id$X$ and $f \circ g \simeq$ id$Y$ where id$X: X \to X$ and id$Y: Y \to Y$ are the identity maps of $X$ and $Y$, respectively. Thus, by Lemma 7.37, there is a path $\alpha: I \to X$ in $X$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and a path $\beta: I \to Y$ from $y_0$ to $y_1$ such that $(g\circ(f_{x 0} ))* = g* \circ(f_{x 0} )* = \alpha \circ$ (id$X )* = \alpha$ and $\hat{Y} )* = \hat{\beta} ((f_{x 1} )\circ g)* = (f_{x 1} )* \circ g* = \beta\circ$ (id where we have specified the basepoint for the homomorphisms induced by $f$. In other words, the following two diagrams commute. (id$X )* g* \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \pi_1 (Y, y_0 ) \pi_1 (X, x_1 ) \alpha (\hat{f}_{x 1} )* g* \circ(f_{x 0} )* \hat{\beta} \pi_1 (X, x_1 ) \pi_1 (Y, y_1 )$ Since $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\beta}$ are both isomorphisms, it follows by commutativity of the diagram on the left that $g*$ is an epimorphism, and by commutativity of the diagram on the right that $g*$ is a monomorphism. Hence, $g*$ is an isomorphism. Since $(f_{x 0} )* = (g* )^{-1} \circ \hat{\alpha}$ it follows that $(f_{x 0} )*$ is an isomorphism.

content.statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. Any continuous $H\colon I\times I\to B$ with $H(0,0)=b_0$ has a unique lift $\tilde H\colon I\times I\to E$ with $\tilde H(0,0)=e_0$; if $H$ is a path homotopy so is $\tilde H$.
content.intuition: Whole homotopies lift uniquely, and path homotopies lift to path homotopies. This makes the lifting correspondence well-defined on $\pi_1$.
content.gloss: Whole homotopies lift uniquely, and path homotopies lift to path homotopies. This makes the lifting correspondence well-defined on $\pi_1$.

examples.tex: Ensures path-homotopic loops in $B$ have lifts with the same endpoint, so $\Phi$ is well-defined (Def. 8.14).

steps.content: We prove the statement about path homotopies. The proof of the first part of the theorem is similar to the proof of Theorem 8.12, where we make use of Lemma 8.10 to break $I \times I$ into smaller squares $[s_{i-1}, s_i ] \times [t_{i-1}, t_i ]$ that are mapped by $H$ into open sets of $B$ that are evenly covered by $p$. See [4, pp. 343–344] for details. Assume that $H$ is a path homotopy from the path $f_0: I \to B$ to the path $f_1: I \to B$ where $f_0 (0) = f_1 (0) = b_0$ and $f_0 (1) = f_1 (1) = b_1$. Then the map $H_0: I \to B$ given by $H_0 (t) = H(0, t) 0: \tilde{I} \to E$ given by is the constant path in $B$ at $b_0$. Hence, the map $H 0 (\tilde{t}) = H(0, H \tilde{t})$ is a lift of $H_0$ where $H 0 (0) = \tilde{H}(0, 0) = e_0$. Note that the constant path $c_{e 0}: I \to E$ at $e_0$ is also a lift of $H_0$ such that $c_{e 0} (0) = e_0$. Thus by uniqueness $H 0 = \tilde{c}_e$. In other words, $\tilde{H}(0, t) = e_0$ for all $t \in I$. Similarly, let $H_1: I \to B$ be the map given by $H_1 (t) = H(1, t). 0) = \tilde{e}_1$. Then the map $H$ Then $H_1$ is the constant path $c_{b 1}$ in $B$ at $b_1$. Let $H(1, 1: \tilde{I} \to E$ given by $1 (\tilde{t}) = \tilde{H} H(1, t)$ is a lift of $H_1$ where $H 1 (0) = \tilde{H}(1, 0) = \tilde{e}_1$. As the constant path $c_e: I \to E$ at $e_1$ is also a lift of $1 = \tilde{c}_e$. In other words, $H(1, H_1$ such that $c_{e 1} (0) = e_1$, it follows by uniqueness that $H \tilde{t}) = e_1$ for all $t \in I$. Thus $H$ is a path homotopy from $\tilde{f}_0$ to $\tilde{f}_1$.

content.statement: Spaces $X,Y$ have the same \textbf{homotopy type} (are \textbf{homotopy equivalent}) if there is a homotopy equivalence $f\colon X\to Y$.
content.intuition: The equivalence relation under which topology classifies spaces ‘up to deformation’. Many invariants, including $\pi_1$, depend only on homotopy type.
content.gloss: The equivalence relation under which topology classifies spaces ‘up to deformation’. Many invariants, including $\pi_1$, depend only on homotopy type.

examples.tex: All contractible spaces (those homotopy equivalent to a point) share one homotopy type.

content.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $A\subseteq X$, the image of $A$ is $f(A)=\{f(x)\mid x\in A\}$. The image of $f$ is $f(X)$.
content.definition: The subset of the codomain attained by applying a function to a subset of the domain.
content.formal: $f(A)=\{y\in Y:\exists x\in A,\ f(x)=y\}$.
content.intuition: What the function actually hits.
content.gloss: All values actually attained by $f$. $f$ is surjective exactly when $f(A)=B$.

examples.tex: For $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, the image is $[0,\infty)$.

content.statement: For $A\subseteq X$, the inclusion map $i:A\hookrightarrow X$ is $i(a)=a$.
content.definition: The canonical function viewing a subset as part of its ambient set.
content.formal: $i:A\to X$, $i(a)=a$.
content.intuition: It does nothing to points; it only changes the ambient viewpoint.
content.gloss: The map that views a subset as sitting inside the ambient set. Composing with it recovers a restriction: $f\circ i=f|_R$.

examples.tex: The inclusion of a subspace is continuous for the subspace topology.

content.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{indiscrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}=\{\varnothing,X\}$.
content.intuition: The coarsest possible topology — the only open sets are the empty set and the whole space. Points are topologically indistinguishable.
content.gloss: The coarsest possible topology — the only open sets are the empty set and the whole space. Points are topologically indistinguishable.

examples.tex: For any topology $\mathcal{T}$ on $X$ we have $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Every map \emph{into} an indiscrete space is continuous.

content.statement: A based map $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ induces $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ by $h_*([f])=[h\circ f]$; it is a group homomorphism (Lemma 7.23).
content.intuition: Maps of spaces turn into maps of fundamental groups by post-composing loops. This is how $\pi_1$ becomes algebraic.
content.gloss: Maps of spaces turn into maps of fundamental groups by post-composing loops. This is how $\pi_1$ becomes algebraic.

examples.tex: $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*$ is the identity; composites behave well (functoriality, Thm. 7.24).

content.statement: $A\cap B=\{x\mid x\in A\text{ and }x\in B\}$ and $A\cup B=\{x\mid x\in A\text{ or }x\in B\}$. Arbitrary unions and finite intersections are part of the definition of a topology.
content.definition: Set operations that combine collections by pooling elements or retaining common elements.
content.formal: $\bigcup_{i\in I}A_i=\{x:\exists i\in I, x\in A_i\}$ and $\bigcap_{i\in I}A_i=\{x:\forall i\in I, x\in A_i\}$.
content.intuition: Union glues open pieces together; intersection checks simultaneous membership.
content.gloss: Intersection keeps common elements; union pools both. They are commutative, associative, and distribute over each other.

examples.tex: $A,B$ are \emph{disjoint} when $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Always $A\cap B\subseteq A\subseteq A\cup B$.

content.statement: If $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ are topologies on $X$, then $\mathcal{T}_1\cap\mathcal{T}_2$ is a topology. More generally $\bigcap_{\lambda}\mathcal{T}_\lambda$ is a topology for any family.
content.intuition: Topologies are closed under intersection — the basis for “generating” the smallest topology containing a given collection. The \emph{union} of topologies generally is not a topology.
content.gloss: Topologies are closed under intersection — the basis for “generating” the smallest topology containing a given collection. The \emph{union} of topologies generally is not a topology.

examples.tex: On $\{a,b,c\}$, intersecting two topologies can give $\{\varnothing,X\}$ while their union fails T2.

steps.content: Clearly, $\varnothing$ and $X$ are in $\mathcal{T}_1 \cap \mathcal{T}_2$, so T1 is satisfied. Let $\{U_\lambda \}\lambda\in\Lambda$ be a collection of sets such that $U_\lambda \in \mathcal{T}_1 \cap \mathcal{T}_2$ for each $\lambda \in \Lambda$ where $\Lambda$ is some $\bigcup$

content.statement: An \textbf{isomorphism} is a bijective homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$. An isomorphism from a group to itself is an \textbf{automorphism}.
content.intuition: A perfect structural match between groups: same group, relabelled. Isomorphic groups are interchangeable for all group-theoretic purposes.
content.gloss: A perfect structural match between groups: same group, relabelled. Isomorphic groups are interchangeable for all group-theoretic purposes.

examples.tex: $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an isomorphism (Thm. 8.16).

content.statement: For a compact metric space $(X,d)$ and an open cover $\mathcal{A}$, there is $\lambda>0$ (a \textbf{Lebesgue number}) such that every ball $\mathsf{B}(x;\lambda)$ lies inside some single $U\in\mathcal{A}$.
content.intuition: On a compact metric space, every open cover has a uniform scale below which small balls fit inside one cover element. Enables subdividing paths finely enough to lift.
content.gloss: On a compact metric space, every open cover has a uniform scale below which small balls fit inside one cover element. Enables subdividing paths finely enough to lift.

examples.tex: Used to chop $I$ into small subintervals, each mapping into one evenly covered open set (proof of Thm. 8.12).

steps.content: If $X \in \mathcal{A}$, then every real number $\lambda > 0$ will be a Lebesgue number for $\mathcal{A}$. So assume that $X$ is not an element of $\mathcal{A}$. By compactness, there is a finite subcollection, say, $\{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n \}$ of $\mathcal{A}$ that covers $X$. For each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, let $V_i = X \setminus U_i$. Then each $V_i$ is non-empty as $U_i \neq X$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Let $\mathbb{R}$ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology (and the standard Euclidean metric), and let $f: X \to \mathbb{R}$ be given by $n 1\sum f (x) =$ dist $V_i (x) n i=1$ where dist $V_i: X \to \mathbb{R}$ is given by dist $V_i (x) =$ inf $\{d(x, v) \mid v \in V_i \}$. We claim that dist $V_i$ is continuous for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be two points in $X$. Then by M3, we have dist $V_i (x_1 ) \leq d(x_1, v) \leq d(x_1, x_2 ) + d(x_2, v)$, for all $v \in V_i$, and so, dist $V_i (x_1 ) - d(x_1, x_2 ) \leq$ dist $V_i (x_2 )$. Similarly, we have dist $V_i (x_2 ) - d(x_1, x_2 ) \leq$ dist $V_i (x_1 )$. Hence, | dist $V_i (x_1 ) -$ dist $V_i (x_2 )| \leq d(x_1, x_2 )$. Thus dist $V_i$ is continuous for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Hence, $f$ is also continuous.

content.statement: For $H\le G$ and $g\in G$: the \textbf{left coset} is $gH=\{g*h\mid h\in H\}$ and the \textbf{right coset} is $Hg=\{h*g\mid h\in H\}$.
content.intuition: Translates of a subgroup. Cosets partition $G$ into equal-sized blocks; they coincide ($gH=Hg$) precisely for normal subgroups.
content.gloss: Translates of a subgroup. Cosets partition $G$ into equal-sized blocks; they coincide ($gH=Hg$) precisely for normal subgroups.

examples.tex: In an abelian group all left and right cosets agree.

content.statement: Given $p\colon E\to B$ and $f\colon X\to B$, a \textbf{lifting} of $f$ is a continuous $\tilde f\colon X\to E$ with $p\circ\tilde f=f$.
content.intuition: Lift a map from the base into the total space, compatibly with $p$. Path and homotopy lifting (for covering maps) drive the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.
content.gloss: Lift a map from the base into the total space, compatibly with $p$. Path and homotopy lifting (for covering maps) drive the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.

examples.tex: A path in $S^1$ lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ once a starting point is chosen (Thm. 8.12).

content.statement: For a covering $p\colon E\to B$ with $p(e_0)=b_0$, the \textbf{lifting correspondence} is $\Phi\colon\pi_1(B,b_0)\to p^{-1}(b_0)$, $\Phi([f])=\tilde f(1)$, where $\tilde f$ is the lift of the loop $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
content.intuition: Send each loop class to the endpoint of its lift in the fibre. Well-defined by homotopy lifting; a bijection when $E$ is path-connected (Thm. 8.15).
content.gloss: Send each loop class to the endpoint of its lift in the fibre. Well-defined by homotopy lifting; a bijection when $E$ is path-connected (Thm. 8.15).

examples.tex: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $\Phi$ sends a loop to its winding number, giving $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

content.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is a \textbf{local homeomorphism} if every $x\in X$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $f(U)$ open and $f|_U\colon U\to f(U)$ a homeomorphism.
content.intuition: Looks like a homeomorphism in a neighborhood of each point, though not necessarily globally. Every covering map is a local homeomorphism.
content.gloss: Looks like a homeomorphism in a neighborhood of each point, though not necessarily globally. Every covering map is a local homeomorphism.

examples.tex: Preserves local properties such as local connectedness; $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ is a local homeomorphism but not injective.

content.statement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $x\in X$, and let $A\subseteq X$. $A$ is a \textbf{neighborhood} of $x$ if it contains some open ball centered at $x$. It is an \textbf{open neighborhood} of $x$ if, in addition, $A$ is open.
content.definition: In a metric space $(X,d)$, a subset $A\subseteq X$ is a neighborhood of $x\in X$ if there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that the open ball $B_d(x,\varepsilon)$ is contained in $A$.
content.formal: $$\begin{aligned} A\in\mathcal N_X(x) &\Longleftrightarrow \exists\varepsilon>0\text{ such that }B_d(x,\varepsilon)\subseteq A,\\ B_d(x,\varepsilon) &=\{y\in X\mid d(x,y)<\varepsilon\}. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: A neighborhood of $x$ is any set with positive metric breathing room around $x$. The set itself need not be open; it only has to contain a small open ball centered at $x$.
content.gloss: Metric version of the broad neighborhood convention: $A$ may be non-open, provided it contains some open ball around $x$. If $A$ itself is open, it is an open neighborhood.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb R$, $[-1,2]$ is a neighborhood of $0$ because $(-1/2,1/2)\subseteq[-1,2]$. It is not a neighborhood of $2$, since no open interval centered at $2$ lies inside $[-1,2]$.

content.statement: Let $(X,\mathcal{T})$ be a topological space and let $U\subseteq X$. Then $$\begin{aligned} U\in\mathcal{T} &\iff \forall x\in U,\ \exists U_x\in\mathcal{T}\text{ such that }x\in U_x\subseteq U \\ &\iff U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x\text{ for some choice of open sets }U_x\in\mathcal{T}\text{ with }x\in U_x\subseteq U. \end{aligned}$$ Equivalently, $U$ is open exactly when every point of $U$ has an open neighborhood contained in $U$.
content.intuition: A set is open exactly when every point inside it has some open neighborhood still contained in the set. If $U$ is already open, choose $U_x=U$ for every $x\in U$. Conversely, if such neighborhoods $U_x$ exist, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$, so $U$ is open.
content.gloss: A set is open exactly when every point inside it has some open neighborhood still contained in the set. If $U$ is already open, choose $U_x=U$ for every $x\in U$. Conversely, if such neighborhoods $U_x$ exist, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$, so $U$ is open.

examples.tex: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, $(0,1)$ is open because every point $x\in(0,1)$ has a smaller open interval around it contained in $(0,1)$. The set $[0,1]$ is not open in $\mathbb{R}$ because $0$ has no open neighborhood contained in $[0,1]$.

steps.content: If $U$ is open and $x\in U$, then $U$ itself is an open neighborhood of $x$ contained in $U$.
Conversely, if every $x\in U$ lies in an open set $U_x\subseteq U$, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$.
Since arbitrary unions of open sets are open, $U$ is open.

content.statement: $H\le G$ is \textbf{normal} if $gH=Hg$ for all $g$; equivalently $g*h*g^{-1}\in H$ for all $g\in G$, $h\in H$.
content.intuition: A subgroup whose left and right cosets agree — exactly the condition needed for the cosets to form a group.
content.gloss: A subgroup whose left and right cosets agree — exactly the condition needed for the cosets to form a group.

examples.tex: Every subgroup of an abelian group is normal; kernels of homomorphisms are always normal.

content.statement: A map $f$ is \textbf{nullhomotopic} if $f\simeq c$ for some constant map $c$.
content.intuition: The map can be continuously shrunk to a single point. Loops being nullhomotopic is what makes a space simply connected.
content.gloss: The map can be continuously shrunk to a single point. Loops being nullhomotopic is what makes a space simply connected.

examples.tex: Every map into $\mathbb{R}^n$ is nullhomotopic; the identity $S^1\to S^1$ is \emph{not}.

content.statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{open} if every $a\in A$ has an open ball $\mathsf{B}(a;r)\subseteq A$. It is \textbf{closed} if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
content.intuition: Open means every point sits inside the set with a little room to spare. Closed is defined by its complement. Most sets are neither; sets that are both are called \emph{clopen}.
content.gloss: Open means every point sits inside the set with a little room to spare. Closed is defined by its complement. Most sets are neither; sets that are both are called \emph{clopen}.

examples.tex: Open balls are open and closed balls are closed (Lemma 2.10). Both $\varnothing$ and $X$ are clopen.

content.statement: For $(X,d)$, $a\in X$ and $r>0$: the \textbf{open ball} is $\mathsf{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$ and the \textbf{closed ball} is $\overline{\mathsf{B}}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\le r\}$.
content.intuition: All points within distance $r$ of a centre $a$ — strictly less than $r$ for open, at most $r$ for closed. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ these are ordinary balls and discs.
content.gloss: All points within distance $r$ of a centre $a$ — strictly less than $r$ for open, at most $r$ for closed. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ these are ordinary balls and discs.

examples.tex: In the discrete metric, $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $r\le 1$ and $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.

content.statement: A continuous $f\colon X\to Y$ is an \textbf{open map} if $f(U)$ is open for every open $U$, and a \textbf{closed map} if $f(V)$ is closed for every closed $V$.
content.intuition: These push openness/closedness \emph{forward}, unlike continuity which pulls openness back. They are the tool linking quotient maps to homeomorphisms.
content.gloss: These push openness/closedness \emph{forward}, unlike continuity which pulls openness back. They are the tool linking quotient maps to homeomorphisms.

examples.tex: Every homeomorphism is both open and closed, but $\mathbb{R}\to\{*\}$ is open and closed yet not a homeomorphism.

content.statement: Let $X=A\cup B$ with $A,B$ closed in $X$, and let $f\colon A\to Y$, $g\colon B\to Y$ be continuous with $f=g$ on $A\cap B$. Then $h\colon X\to Y$ defined piecewise by $f$ and $g$ is continuous.
content.intuition: Two continuous pieces that agree on the overlap glue to a continuous whole — provided the pieces are closed.
content.gloss: Two continuous pieces that agree on the overlap glue to a continuous whole — provided the pieces are closed.

examples.tex: Makes the concatenation of paths $f*g$ continuous (Def. 7.12).

steps.content: To prove $h$ is continuous, test preimages of closed subsets of $Y$. Let $C\subseteq Y$ be closed.
Because $h$ restricts to $f$ on $A$ and to $g$ on $B$, one has $h^{-1}(C)=f^{-1}(C)\cup g^{-1}(C)$.
The sets $f^{-1}(C)$ and $g^{-1}(C)$ are closed in the closed pieces $A$ and $B$, hence closed in $X$. Their finite union is closed, so $h$ is continuous.

content.statement: Paths $f,g\colon I\to X$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ are \textbf{path homotopic} ($f\simeq_p g$) if there is continuous $H\colon I\times I\to X$ with $H(s,0)=f(s)$, $H(s,1)=g(s)$, and $H(0,t)=x_0$, $H(1,t)=x_1$ for all $t$.
content.intuition: Deform one path into another while keeping the endpoints pinned. This rel-endpoints relation is what the fundamental group is built from.
content.gloss: Deform one path into another while keeping the endpoints pinned. This rel-endpoints relation is what the fundamental group is built from.

examples.tex: Any two paths in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the same endpoints are path homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

content.statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$, $[f]=\{g\mid g\ \text{a path}\ x_0\to x_1,\ f\simeq_p g\}$ is its \textbf{path homotopy class}.
content.intuition: The endpoint-fixed deformation class of a path. The fundamental group consists of such classes for loops.
content.gloss: The endpoint-fixed deformation class of a path. The fundamental group consists of such classes for loops.

examples.tex: The product $*$ and inverse descend to these classes, giving group structure (Thm. 7.14).

content.statement: Every path-connected space is connected.
content.intuition: Being walkable implies being in one piece. The converse fails in general, though it holds for open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$.
content.gloss: Being walkable implies being in one piece. The converse fails in general, though it holds for open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$.

examples.tex: The topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected (a standard counterexample).

steps.content: Assume $X$ is path-connected but disconnected, and let $U,V$ be a separation of $X$.
Pick $x\in U$ and $y\in V$. Path-connectedness gives a path $f:[a,b]\to X$ from $x$ to $y$.
The sets $f^{-1}(U)$ and $f^{-1}(V)$ are disjoint nonempty open subsets whose union is $[a,b]$, contradicting connectedness of intervals.

concepts.label: Product of Compact Spaces Is Compact

content.statement: If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are compact, then $X_1\times\cdots\times X_n$ is compact.
content.intuition: Compactness survives finite products (and, by Tychonoff, arbitrary products with the product topology).
content.gloss: Compactness survives finite products (and, by Tychonoff, arbitrary products with the product topology).

examples.tex: With Thm. 6.40 this gives compactness of closed boxes in $\mathbb{R}^n$, hence Heine–Borel.

steps.content: It suffices to prove the two-factor case; the finite product case follows by induction.
For an open cover of $X\times Y$, each slice $\{x\}\times Y$ is compact and therefore is covered by finitely many cover elements.
The tube lemma gives a neighborhood $W_x$ of $x$ such that $W_x\times Y$ is covered by the same finite subcollection.
The neighborhoods $W_x$ cover $X$. Choose finitely many using compactness of $X$; the corresponding finite cover elements cover all of $X\times Y$.

content.statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and $g$ from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the \textbf{product} $f*g\colon I\to X$ is $f$ run on $[0,\tfrac12]$ then $g$ on $[\tfrac12,1]$: $(f*g)(s)=f(2s)$ for $s\le\tfrac12$, $g(2s-1)$ otherwise.
content.intuition: Concatenate two paths by traversing the first at double speed, then the second. Continuous by the pasting lemma.
content.gloss: Concatenate two paths by traversing the first at double speed, then the second. Continuous by the pasting lemma.

examples.tex: On path homotopy classes, $*$ is associative with constant-path units and reverse-path inverses (Thm. 7.14).

content.statement: For a normal subgroup $H\trianglelefteq G$, the cosets $G/H$ form a group under $(g_1H)(g_2H)=(g_1g_2)H$.
content.intuition: Collapse a normal subgroup to the identity: the cosets inherit a well-defined group structure. The algebraic analogue of a quotient space.
content.gloss: Collapse a normal subgroup to the identity: the cosets inherit a well-defined group structure. The algebraic analogue of a quotient space.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}$ is the cyclic group of order $m$.

steps.content: We first show that the binary operation defined on $G/H$ is well-defined. Let $g_1' \in g_1 H$, and let $g_2' \in g_2 H$. Then $g_1 H = g_1' H$ and $g_2 H = g_2' H$. We must show that $(g_1 H)(g_2 H) = (g_1' H)(g_2' H)$. Choose an element $(g_1 g_2 )h \in (g_1 g_2 )H$. Since $g_1 \in g_1 H = g_1' H$ and $g_2 \in g_2 H = g_2' H$, there are elements $h_1$ and $h_2$ in $H$ such that $g_1 = g_1' h_1$ and $g_2 = g_2' h_2$. Thus $(g_1 g_2 )h = (g_1' h_1 )(g_2' h_2 )h = g_1' h_1 g_2' h_2 h = g_1' (g_2' (g_2' )^{-1} )h_1 g_2' h_2 h = g_1' g_2' ((g_2' )^{-1} h_1 g_2' )h_2 h = g_1' g_2' h'1 h_2 h \in (g_1' g_2' )H$ Appendix B. Elementary algebra 105 where the last equality follows from the fact $H$ is a normal subgroup of $G$; there is an $h'1 \in H$ such that $(g_2' )^{-1} h_1 g_2 = h'1$. Hence, $(g_1 g_2 )H \subseteq (g_1' g_2' )H$. A similar argument shows that $(g_1' g_2' )H \subseteq (g_1 g_2 )H$. Hence, $(g_1 g_2 )H = (g_1' g_2' )H$. Thus $(g_1 H)(g_2 H) = (g_1 g_2 )H = (g_1' g_2' )H = (g_1' H)(g_2' H)$. We now show that $G/H$ is a group. Let $g_1 H, g_2 H$ and $g_2 H$ be elements in $G/H$. We must show that $[(g_1 H)(g_2 H)](g_3 H) = (g_1 H)[(g_2 H)(g_3 H)]$. By definition, we have $[(g_1 H)(g_2 H)](g_3 H) = (g_1 g_2 H)(g_3 H) = (g_1 g_2 )g_3 H = g_1 (g_2 g_3 )H = (g_1 H)(g_2 g_3 H) = (g_1 H)[(g_2 H)(g_3 H)]$. Hence, G1 is satisfied. If $e$ is the identity element in $G$, then $(g_H)(e_H) = (g_e)H = g_H = (e_g)H = (e_H)(g_H)$ for all $g \in G$. Hence, G2 is satisfied with $e_H = H$ as the identity element in $G/H$. Finally, for each $g \in G$, we have $(g_H)(g^{-1} H) = (g_{g-1} H) = e_H = (g^{-1} g)H = (g^{-1} H)(g_H)$. Hence, G3 is satisfied with $(g_H)^{-1} = g^{-1} H$. Thus $G/H$ is a group.

content.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $A\subseteq X$, the restriction $f|_A:A\to Y$ is the same rule as $f$ but with domain reduced to $A$.
content.definition: A function obtained by limiting the domain of another function.
content.formal: $f|_A(a)=f(a)$ for every $a\in A$.
content.intuition: Use the same map on a smaller input set.
content.gloss: Use the same rule on a smaller domain. Restrictions appear constantly, e.g. $p|_{V_\lambda}$ in covering maps.

examples.tex: If $f|_R$ is injective, then $f|_R\colon R\to f(R)$ is a bijection.

content.statement: For a subspace $A\subseteq X$, a continuous $r\colon X\to A$ is a \textbf{retraction} if $r(a)=a$ for all $a\in A$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{retract} of $X$.
content.intuition: A continuous way of collapsing $X$ onto $A$ that leaves $A$ fixed. Retractions constrain how $\pi_1(A)$ sits inside $\pi_1(X)$.
content.gloss: A continuous way of collapsing $X$ onto $A$ that leaves $A$ fixed. Retractions constrain how $\pi_1(A)$ sits inside $\pi_1(X)$.

examples.tex: $S^1$ is a retract of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ via $r(x)=x/\lVert x\rVert$.

content.statement: A path-connected space $X$ is \textbf{simply connected} if $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is trivial for some (hence every) $x_0\in X$.
content.intuition: Path-connected and every loop contracts to a point — no $1$-dimensional holes.
content.gloss: Path-connected and every loop contracts to a point — no $1$-dimensional holes.

examples.tex: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is simply connected; $S^1$ and the torus $T^2$ are not.

content.statement: A subset $H\subseteq G$ is a \textbf{subgroup} ($H\le G$) if $*$ restricts to a binary operation on $H$ making $(H,*|_{H\times H})$ a group. It is \textbf{proper} ($H<G$) if $H\subsetneq G$.
content.intuition: A subset that is itself a group under the same operation. The trivial subgroup $\{e\}$ always qualifies.
content.gloss: A subset that is itself a group under the same operation. The trivial subgroup $\{e\}$ always qualifies.

examples.tex: $m\mathbb{Z}=\{mn\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a proper subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$ for $m\ge 2$.

content.statement: $B$ is a subset of $A$, written $B\subseteq A$, if every element of $B$ is an element of $A$. If $B\subseteq A$ and $B\ne A$, then $B$ is a proper subset of $A$.
content.definition: The inclusion relation between sets.
content.formal: $B\subseteq A\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\,(x\in B\Rightarrow x\in A)$.
content.intuition: All points of the smaller set are already inside the larger set.
content.gloss: Everything in $B$ is already in $A$. Sets are equal iff each is a subset of the other.

examples.tex: $\{1,2\}\subsetneq\{1,2,3\}$ and $\mathbb{Z}\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.

content.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is injective if distinct inputs have distinct outputs.
content.definition: A function with no collisions: different inputs map to different outputs.
content.formal: $f(x_1)=f(x_2)\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$.
content.intuition: No two distinct points in the domain get sent to the same point in the codomain.
content.gloss: No two distinct points in the domain get sent to the same point in the codomain.

examples.tex: $f(x)=2x$ is injective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

content.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is surjective if every element of $Y$ is hit.
content.definition: A function with no missed targets: every point in the codomain has a preimage.
content.formal: $\forall y\in Y\,\exists x\in X:f(x)=y$.
content.intuition: Every point in the codomain gets hit by at least one point in the domain.
content.gloss: Every point in the codomain gets hit by at least one point in the domain.

examples.tex: $f(x)=x$ is surjective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

content.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is bijective if it is both injective and surjective.
content.definition: A function that is both injective and surjective: a perfect one-to-one correspondence between the domain and codomain.
content.formal: $f:X\to Y$ is bijective iff for every $y\in Y$ there exists a unique $x\in X$ such that $f(x)=y$.
content.intuition: Every point in the codomain gets hit by exactly one point in the domain.
content.gloss: A function that is both injective and surjective: a perfect one-to-one correspondence between the domain and codomain.

examples.tex: $f(x)=x$ is bijective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

content.statement: $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology is connected.
content.intuition: The fundamental connected space. The proof uses the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$.
content.gloss: The fundamental connected space. The proof uses the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$.

examples.tex: Consequently all intervals are connected, and $\mathbb{R}^n$ is connected.

steps.content: Assume $\mathbb{R}$ is separated by nonempty disjoint open sets $U$ and $V$, and choose $a\in U$, $b\in V$ with $a<b$.
The sets $A=[a,b]\cap U$ and $B=[a,b]\cap V$ separate $[a,b]$, with $a\in A$ and $b\in B$.
Let $c=\sup A$. Openness of $B$ rules out $c\in B$, and openness of $A$ rules out $c\in A$.
Since $c\in[a,b]$ but $c$ belongs to neither side of the separation, contradiction. Therefore $\mathbb{R}$ is connected.

content.statement: A \textbf{topological property} is any property expressible in terms of points and open sets; such properties are preserved by homeomorphisms. A \textbf{topological invariant} is a quantity invariant under homeomorphism.
content.intuition: These are exactly the features topology can see. If $X\cong Y$ and $X$ has the property, so does $Y$ — hence invariants can prove two spaces are \emph{not} homeomorphic.
content.gloss: These are exactly the features topology can see. If $X\cong Y$ and $X$ has the property, so does $Y$ — hence invariants can prove two spaces are \emph{not} homeomorphic.

examples.tex: Connectedness, compactness, Hausdorffness and the fundamental group are all topological invariants.

content.statement: For a collection $\mathcal{S}$ of subsets of $X$, the \textbf{topology generated by} $\mathcal{S}$ is $\langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\bigcap\{\mathcal{T}\ \text{topology}\mid \mathcal{S}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\}$, the smallest topology containing $\mathcal{S}$.
content.intuition: Take any collection of “intended open sets” and form the coarsest topology making them open, by intersecting all topologies that contain them.
content.gloss: Take any collection of “intended open sets” and form the coarsest topology making them open, by intersecting all topologies that contain them.

examples.tex: $\langle\varnothing\rangle=\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}$; the collection of all singletons generates $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$.

content.statement: Let $Y$ be compact. If $x\in X$ and $U$ is open in $X\times Y$ containing the slice $\{x\}\times Y$, then there is a neighborhood $W$ of $x$ with $W\times Y\subseteq U$.
content.intuition: An open set containing a whole compact slice automatically contains a “tube” $W\times Y$ around it. The technical engine behind compactness of products.
content.gloss: An open set containing a whole compact slice automatically contains a “tube” $W\times Y$ around it. The technical engine behind compactness of products.

examples.tex: Used directly to prove that a finite product of compact spaces is compact (Thm. 6.39).

steps.content: For each $y\in Y$, choose a basic product neighborhood $W_y\times V_y\subseteq U$ containing $(x,y)$.
The sets $V_y$ cover $Y$, so compactness gives a finite subcover $V_{y_1},\ldots,V_{y_n}$.
Let $W=W_{y_1}\cap\cdots\cap W_{y_n}$. Then $W$ is a neighborhood of $x$ and $W\times Y\subseteq U$.

content.statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. For any path $f\colon I\to B$ with $f(0)=b_0$ there is a \textbf{unique} path $\tilde f\colon I\to E$ lifting $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
content.intuition: Paths lift uniquely once you fix a starting point upstairs. The cornerstone for relating loops in $B$ to points in a fibre.
content.gloss: Paths lift uniquely once you fix a starting point upstairs. The cornerstone for relating loops in $B$ to points in a fibre.

examples.tex: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, a loop winding once lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ from $0$ to $1$.

steps.content:
For each $s \in I$, let $U_s$ be a neighborhood of $f (s)$ that is evenly covered by $p$. The collection $\mathcal{A} = \{f^{-1} (U_s ) \mid s \in I\}$ is then an open cover of $I$. Since $I$ is a compact metric space, it follows from Lemma 8.10 that there is a Lebesgue number $\lambda > 0$ for $\mathcal{A}$. Consider a subdivision of $I$ consisting of points 0 = $s_0 < s_1 < \cdots < s_n = 1$ such that $s_i - s_{i-1} < 2\lambda$ for 1 $\leq i \leq n$. Then each interval $[s_{i-1}, s_i ]$ lies in one of the preimages $f^{-1} (U_s )$. Thus $f ([s_{i-1}, s_i ]) \subseteq U_s$ for some $s \in I$. Continuing this process, we get a continuous map ( )$-1 	ilde{g}_i = p|V_\omega \circ f |[s_{i-1},s_i ]: [s_{i-1}, s_i ] 	o E i^{-1} g_i = f |[s_{i-1},s_i ]$ and and where $p\circ 	ilde{	ilde{g}}_i (s_{i-1} ) = 	ilde{g}_i^{-1} (s_{i-1} ) = e_{i-1}$ for $i \in \{3, 4, \ldots, n\}$. By Lemma 7.3, we can combine $	ilde{g}_1, 	ilde{g}_2, \ldots, 	ilde{g}_n$ into a continuous map $	ilde{f}: I 	o E$ given by $	ilde{f}(s) = 	ilde{g}_i (s)$ for $s \in [s_{i-1}, s_i ]$. Assume that there are two continuous maps $	ilde{f}_1: I 	o E$ and $	ilde{f}_2: I 	o E$ such that $p\circ	ilde{f}_1 = p\circ	ilde{f}_2 = f$ and $	ilde{f}_1 (0) = 	ilde{f}_2 (0) = e_0$. We want to show that $	ilde{f}_1 = 	ilde{f}_2$. Let $A = \{s \in I \mid 	ilde{f}_1 (s) = 	ilde{f}_2 (s)\}$ and $D = \{s \in I \mid 	ilde{f}_1 (s) 
eq 	ilde{f}_2 (s)\}$. Then clearly, $I = A \cup D$ and $A \cap D = \varnothing$. If we can show that both $A$ are $D$ are open, then because $I$ is connected we have $A = I$ and $D = \varnothing$ as 0 $\in A$, and hence, $	ilde{f}_1 = 	ilde{f}_2$. Let $s \in A$, and let $U$ be a neighborhood of $f (s)$ that is evenly covered by $p$. Assume that $V_\omega$ is the subset of $p^{-1} (U )$ that contains $	ilde{f}_1 (s) = 	ilde{f}_2 (s)$.
